Core Meaning: A distinct, scheduled portion of work performed during the course of a project.
Expansion: An activity is a unit of scheduled work used to plan, sequence, assign, and control execution within a project. It is more specific than a broad deliverable and is usually linked to duration, resources, dependencies, and progress tracking. Activities are the building blocks of schedules and are central to forecasting completion dates and managing coordination across the project.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An activity is a defined piece of work that can be scheduled and managed. In scheduling practice, activities are sequenced, given durations, assigned resources, and monitored for status.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects become manageable when large outcomes are broken into activities that teams can plan and execute. Activities are essential in schedule development, critical path analysis, resource planning, and short-interval control across predictive and hybrid projects.

The most common misconception: that an activity is the same as a deliverable. A deliverable is the output or result, while an activity is the work performed to create that output.

How it shows up in real projects: a construction project may include activities such as pour foundation, install framing, and inspect electrical systems, each with predecessors, durations, and assigned crews.
Contrast: Deliverable — an activity is the work performed; a deliverable is the result produced by that work.
Example: In an office relocation project, 'configure network switches on the new floor' is an activity scheduled after cabling installation and before user move-in.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Activity

Core Meaning: The set of detailed characteristics associated with each schedule activity.
Expansion: Activity attributes extend the activity list by capturing the details needed to build and manage the schedule. They typically include an activity identifier and code, predecessors and successors, logical relationships, leads and lags, resource requirements, imposed dates, constraints, and assumptions. The attributes evolve as planning progresses and support schedule development, reporting, and filtering.
Contrast: Activity list - the activity list simply names the scheduled activities, whereas activity attributes hold the supporting detail for each one.
Example: For the activity 'Pour foundation,' the attributes record its predecessor 'Excavate site,' a 2-day lag for curing, and a crew of three laborers.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Define Activities (activity attributes)

Core Meaning: An alphanumeric value that classifies a schedule activity so activities can be sorted and filtered.
Expansion: An activity code is a numeric or alphanumeric tag assigned to schedule activities to categorize them, for example by phase, location, or responsible department. Codes let planners filter, group, and order activities within schedule reports.
Contrast: Activity identifier - an identifier uniquely names one activity, whereas an activity code is a shared category used to group many activities.
Example: Tagging all electrical activities with the code 'ELEC' so a report can show only electrical work.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule activity attributes

Core Meaning: The estimated number of work periods needed to complete a scheduled activity.
Expansion: Activity duration expresses how long an activity is expected to take once work begins, usually stated in workdays or another agreed unit of time. In PMI usage it is distinct from elapsed calendar time because calendars, holidays, and resource availability affect when work can occur. It is a core input to sequencing, scheduling, and forecasting completion dates.
Contrast: Duration estimate — activity duration is the expected length of the activity itself; a duration estimate is the assessment process or output used to determine that length.
Example: In an office-fit-out project, installing network cabling is assigned an activity duration of four workdays based on crew size and building access windows.
Source: Practice Standard for Scheduling, Second Edition, p. 13

Core Meaning: A quantitative assessment of the likely number of work periods required to complete an activity.
Expansion: An activity duration estimate is the documented estimate for how long a specific scheduled activity should take. PMI treats it as an output of estimating processes that may use expert judgment, analogous estimating, parametric estimating, three-point estimating, or reserve analysis. It supports schedule development and becomes more reliable as scope, resources, and assumptions are clarified.
Contrast: Activity duration — an activity duration estimate is the estimate produced for planning; activity duration is the planned or expected length being represented.
Example: For a data-migration task, the team develops a duration estimate of six to eight workdays using expert judgment and lessons learned from a prior rollout.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 191

Core Meaning: The quantitative assessments of the likely number of work periods needed to complete an activity.
Expansion: Activity duration estimates state how many work periods (hours, days, weeks) an activity is expected to take, given the planned resources. They may be expressed as a single value or as a range with confidence levels, and often note the assumptions behind them. These estimates feed schedule development and are produced by techniques such as analogous, parametric, and three-point estimating.
Contrast: Effort - effort is the number of labor units (e.g., person-hours) required, whereas duration is the elapsed work periods, which depend on how many resources are applied.
Example: Estimating that testing a module will take 5 days, assuming one tester works on it full time.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Estimate Activity Durations

Core Meaning: The process of estimating the number of work periods needed to complete individual schedule activities.
Expansion: Activity duration estimating determines how long planned activities are expected to take under defined conditions. PMI treats it as a core scheduling process because reliable durations are necessary to build schedules, forecast milestones, and assess feasibility. Duration estimates depend not only on effort, but also on resource availability, calendars, constraints, assumptions, and uncertainty.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Activity duration estimating is the practice of determining the expected elapsed time required to complete a schedule activity. It uses inputs such as scope, effort, productivity, resource assignments, historical data, and assumptions.

Why it matters and where it applies: duration estimates directly shape the schedule, affect critical path and float calculations, and influence delivery commitments. They are used in predictive, hybrid, and many adaptive planning contexts whenever teams need to forecast completion timing.

The most common misconception: that duration is the same as effort. Effort is the amount of work required, while duration is the calendar or working time over which that work is completed given the available resources and constraints.

How it shows up in real projects: a testing activity may require 40 hours of effort but have a duration of two weeks because the tester is only available part time and waits for test data and approvals.
Contrast: Effort estimate — activity duration estimating predicts elapsed time to complete an activity, while an effort estimate predicts the amount of labor required.
Example: A project team estimates that user-acceptance testing will take eight working days because business testers are available only in the afternoons.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition — Estimate Activity Durations

Core Meaning: A unique label distinguishing one schedule activity from all others in the schedule.
Expansion: An activity identifier is a short unique code or number assigned to a single schedule activity so it can be referenced unambiguously in network diagrams, reports, and software. Unlike a category code, it points to exactly one activity.
Contrast: Activity code - a code groups many activities by category, whereas an identifier uniquely names one activity.
Example: Labeling 'Install drywall' as activity A1040 in the scheduling tool.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule activity attributes

Core Meaning: A documented tabulation of schedule activities that are required to complete the project.
Expansion: An activity list identifies the distinct schedule activities needed to produce project deliverables and complete project work. It is created during schedule planning and provides the basis for sequencing, estimating, assigning resources, and developing the schedule model. PMI treats it as a core scheduling artifact because work cannot be logically sequenced or duration-estimated until the necessary activities are identified.
Contrast: Work breakdown structure — the WBS decomposes deliverables into work packages, while the activity list identifies the activities needed to complete that work.
Example: For an office fit-out project, the activity list includes tasks such as install partition framing, run electrical wiring, inspect fire systems, and complete final walkthrough.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, page 185

Core Meaning: A diagramming method that shows activities on arrows and connects them through nodes to represent the sequence of work.
Expansion: Activity-on-arrow is a schedule network diagram method in which each activity is represented by an arrow and the nodes show the relationships between activities. It is one of the classic precedence representation approaches used in project scheduling. PMI treats it as a formal network-diagramming technique, though precedence diagramming is more commonly used in practice today.
Contrast: Precedence Diagramming Method — activity-on-arrow places activities on arrows; precedence diagramming places activities in nodes.
Example: A scheduler models a facility upgrade using arrows for excavation, foundation work, framing, and inspection to show the required sequence.

Core Meaning: A diagramming method that places activities in nodes and uses arrows to show logical relationships.
Expansion: Activity-on-node is a schedule network representation in which each activity is shown as a node and arrows indicate precedence relationships. It is closely associated with precedence diagramming and is widely used because it supports different dependency types clearly. PMI recognises it as a standard way to model project schedule logic.
Contrast: Activity-on-arrow — activity-on-node puts activities in boxes or nodes; activity-on-arrow puts activities on arrows.
Example: A software implementation schedule shows requirements, configuration, testing, and deployment as nodes connected by finish-to-start and start-to-start relationships.

Core Meaning: The real elapsed work time an activity has taken, measured from its actual start.
Expansion: Actual duration is the number of work periods between an activity's actual start date and either the data date (for in-progress work) or the actual finish date (for completed work). It is compared against the planned duration to assess schedule performance.
Contrast: Remaining duration - actual duration is time already spent, whereas remaining duration is the time still estimated to finish.
Example: An activity that started Monday and is still running on Thursday's data date has an actual duration of 4 days.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule terms

Core Meaning: The date on which work on a schedule activity actually began.
Expansion: The actual start date records when an activity truly started, as opposed to when it was planned or scheduled to start. Comparing actual and planned start dates supports progress tracking, variance analysis, and schedule forecasting.
Contrast: Early start date — the early start date is the earliest an activity could begin per the schedule model, while the actual start date is when it really began.
Example: An activity planned to start Monday actually began Wednesday, so the project manager records Wednesday as its actual start date and assesses the delay.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — actual start date

Core Meaning: A technique for estimating duration, cost, or resources using historical data from a similar activity or project.
Expansion: Analogous Estimating uses information from past similar work to produce a high-level estimate for current work. It is usually faster and less detailed than bottom-up estimating, which makes it useful early when information is limited. Its accuracy depends on how comparable the prior work really is and how well differences are adjusted.
Contrast: Bottom-up Estimating — Analogous Estimating uses comparison with similar past work for a quicker high-level estimate; Bottom-up Estimating builds the estimate from detailed component estimates.
Example: A project manager estimates a new branch-office fit-out by comparing it with a similar office completed last year and adjusting for the larger floor area.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Analogous Estimating

Core Meaning: A predecessor relationship condition that must be true before a successor activity can begin or finish.
Expansion: Antecedent condition is used in network logic to describe the prior condition that governs a following activity. It helps define sequencing logic in schedule development.
Contrast: Predecessor activity — an antecedent condition is the required prior state or logic condition, while a predecessor activity is the actual activity that creates that condition.
Example: System integration testing cannot start until code completion and environment setup are both satisfied as antecedent conditions.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A calculation that works from the end of the schedule network back to the beginning to determine the late start and late finish dates of activities.
Expansion: The backward pass is used in schedule network analysis to identify how late each activity can occur without delaying the project or a required milestone. It starts with the project completion date or finish milestone and traces dependencies in reverse through the network. The results support float calculations and help identify which activities have scheduling flexibility and which do not.
Contrast: Forward pass — a forward pass calculates the earliest possible dates; a backward pass calculates the latest allowable dates.
Example: In a plant upgrade project, the scheduler starts from the mandated commissioning date and performs a backward pass to find the latest dates each testing and installation activity can begin without moving the deadline.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Glossary, backward pass

Core Meaning: A graphic display that uses horizontal or vertical bars to show values or, in scheduling, activity timing against a timeline.
Expansion: In project management, a bar chart is a visual way to present information clearly and quickly. It is commonly used in schedules, especially in Gantt-style displays, to show when activities start, finish, and overlap. PMI recognises it as a practical communication format for reporting timing or comparative data.
Contrast: Histogram — a bar chart displays categories or schedule activities, while a histogram shows the frequency distribution of numerical data across intervals.
Example: A construction project status report uses a bar chart to show planned and actual completion dates for excavation, framing, and inspection work.
Source: PMI Lexicon / A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Bar chart; exact page citation unavailable in retrieved results

Core Meaning: A method of estimating duration or cost by aggregating estimates from the lowest-level components of the work breakdown structure.
Expansion: Bottom-up estimating starts with detailed estimates for individual work packages or activities and rolls them up to produce a higher-level total. PMI presents it as a more detailed estimating approach than top-down methods because it is built from the work itself rather than from broad analogies. It is useful when the scope is sufficiently defined and the team needs a more defensible estimate for planning and control.
Contrast: Analogous estimating — bottom-up estimating builds from detailed components; analogous estimating derives a figure from similar past work at a higher level.
Example: A software team estimates each feature, integration task, and test activity separately, then sums the results to produce the release estimate.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, bottom-up estimating

Core Meaning: A planned reserve of time, capacity, or other allowance inserted to absorb variability and protect delivery commitments.
Expansion: A buffer is used in project planning to reduce the impact of uncertainty on milestones, handoffs, or constrained resources. In PMI-aligned practice, it is a management device for protecting flow and commitments rather than hidden slack added arbitrarily to every task. Buffers are especially visible in scheduling approaches such as critical chain, but the concept also appears more broadly in contingency planning and operational coordination.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — a buffer is a planned protective allowance embedded in timing or flow, while contingency reserve is budget or schedule capacity set aside for identified risks.
Example: A project team places a two-day integration buffer before user acceptance testing so minor upstream delays do not automatically move the testing start date.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A representation of working and nonworking time that is used to plan and control when project activities and resources are available.
Expansion: In PMI practice, a calendar defines the time periods during which work can occur, such as business days, shifts, holidays, and exceptions. It is used in scheduling to calculate activity dates and resource availability rather than merely to display dates. Different calendars may exist for the project, for resources, or for specific activities when working patterns differ.
Contrast: Schedule — a calendar defines available working time; a schedule places activities and milestones onto that available time.
Example: A construction project uses one calendar for weekday office design work and a different calendar for six-day onsite installation crews.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / earlier PMI scheduling references; exact page citation requires source verification in PMI content

Core Meaning: The smallest unit of time used in scheduling or measuring schedule duration.
Expansion: Calendar units provide the basic time increments used to express activity durations, lags, and reporting periods, such as hours, days, or weeks. Using a consistent unit helps the schedule model remain coherent across planning and control.
Contrast: Work period — a calendar unit is the measurement increment for time, while a work period is a span in the resource or project calendar when work can actually occur.
Example: A project scheduler estimates a design review task as 3 working days, using days as the calendar unit throughout the schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 698

Core Meaning: The discipline of planning, sequencing, and timing construction activities to coordinate labor, materials, equipment, and site access.
Expansion: Construction scheduling translates the project plan into a time-phased sequence that respects physical dependencies, resource limits, and site logistics such as access and weather. It typically relies on network methods and is updated continuously as field progress and changes occur. Coordination across many trades and subcontractors makes timing and handoffs especially critical. Effective schedules drive procurement, cash flow, and claims management on the project.
Contrast: Resource leveling — resource leveling adjusts a schedule to fit resource limits, whereas construction scheduling is the broader domain practice of building and maintaining the timeline.
Example: The contractor sequences foundation, framing, and roofing with a network schedule so each trade has cleared predecessors before mobilizing.
Source: Construction domain practice; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring the status of the project to update the project schedule and manage changes to the schedule baseline.
Expansion: Control schedule compares actual schedule performance with the approved schedule baseline and identifies variances that may require action. It supports forecasting, corrective action, and formal change control when the project is trending away from plan. The process continues throughout delivery and is central to schedule management.
Contrast: Develop schedule — Develop schedule creates the schedule model; Control schedule monitors performance and manages changes to the approved schedule baseline.
Example: A product launch team identifies a two-week testing delay, analyses the impact on downstream milestones, and updates the forecast through schedule control.
Source: PMI Lexicon — control schedule

Core Meaning: A schedule compression technique used to shorten the schedule duration for the least incremental cost by adding resources.
Expansion: Crashing reduces planned duration by applying additional resources to activities, but only where that action can realistically shorten the work. It is typically considered when the project must meet a required finish date or recover from delay on critical-path work. Because it usually increases cost and can raise coordination risk, it must be evaluated carefully rather than applied broadly.
Contrast: Fast tracking — crashing shortens duration by adding resources or cost to activities; fast tracking shortens duration by overlapping activities that were originally planned in sequence.
Example: A software release nearing a fixed compliance deadline adds experienced testers to critical-path system testing to shorten the test window and protect the launch date.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 700

Core Meaning: An activity with zero float that, if delayed, delays the project finish date.
Expansion: A critical activity is an activity on the critical path and therefore has no scheduling flexibility without affecting the planned completion date. PMI uses the concept to identify the work that directly drives schedule duration and requires close monitoring. As logic, durations, or constraints change, the set of critical activities can also change.
Contrast: Critical path — a critical activity is a single activity on the path; the critical path is the full sequence of activities that determines project duration.
Example: In an office relocation project, final network cutover is a critical activity because any delay to it pushes the go-live and occupancy dates.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Critical activity; exact page not available in provided results

Core Meaning: A scheduling method that plans work around resource constraints and protects delivery dates with strategically placed buffers.
Expansion: Critical Chain Method is a schedule-development and control approach derived from the theory of constraints. Instead of focusing only on logical task dependencies, it also accounts for limited resource availability and uses project and feeding buffers to protect the completion date. It is most useful where shared resources and multitasking significantly affect schedule performance.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Critical Chain Method identifies the longest chain of dependent work after considering both task logic and resource constraints. It then protects that chain with buffers rather than padding every activity.

Why it matters and where it applies: many project schedules look feasible on paper until scarce resources are assigned across multiple activities. Critical Chain helps in engineering, product development, and other resource-constrained environments by making schedule risk visible and manageable.

The most common misconception: that it is just the critical path method with extra contingency. In practice, it changes both how the schedule is built and how protection is managed by centralising uncertainty into explicit buffers.

How it shows up in real projects: teams sequence work around key specialists, limit multitasking, monitor buffer consumption, and escalate recovery actions when buffer burn suggests the delivery date is under threat.
Contrast: Critical Path Method — Critical Chain Method accounts explicitly for resource constraints and uses buffers, while Critical Path Method focuses primarily on logical dependencies and float.
Example: A hardware development project reorganises its schedule around a small number of specialist test engineers, adds feeding buffers before the critical chain, and tracks buffer consumption each week to protect the launch date.

Core Meaning: A scheduling method that focuses on resource constraints and protects delivery dates by placing buffers around the work that governs project completion.
Expansion: Critical chain project management modifies schedule planning by considering both task dependencies and resource availability, not just logical sequence. It typically uses feeding buffers and a project buffer to absorb uncertainty and protect the committed finish date. The method is especially relevant where shared resources, multitasking, and optimistic task estimates create schedule instability.
Contrast: Critical path method — critical path focuses on the longest path of dependent activities based mainly on logic and duration, while critical chain also incorporates resource constraints and buffer management.
Example: An engineering project with a small pool of specialist designers sequences work around resource availability and uses a project buffer to protect the final milestone.

Core Meaning: The sequence of activities that represents the longest path through a project and determines the shortest possible project duration.
Expansion: Critical path identifies the activities that directly drive the planned finish date of the project. When one of those activities slips and nothing else changes, the overall project completion date is likely to slip as well. PMI uses it as a core concept in schedule analysis, schedule control, and schedule compression.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The critical path is the longest-duration path through the schedule network, based on logic and activity durations. It indicates the earliest possible completion date under the current plan.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps the team focus attention on the work that most affects delivery timing. It is widely used in predictive scheduling, schedule risk analysis, and decisions about crashing or fast tracking.

The most common misconception: that the critical path is permanent or that only one critical path can exist. In practice it can shift as progress, dependencies, estimates, or constraints change, and some schedules can contain multiple near-critical or critical paths.

How it shows up in real projects: if system testing sits on the critical path and begins to slip, the project manager may add test resources, overlap successor work where acceptable, or revise sequencing to protect the go-live date.
Contrast: Float — critical path identifies the activity sequence that drives project duration; float is the amount of scheduling flexibility available before delay affects a successor or finish date.
Example: In a facility upgrade, procurement of long-lead equipment, installation, commissioning, and final inspection form the critical path because delay in any of them delays handover.

Core Meaning: A method used to estimate the minimum project duration and determine the amount of scheduling flexibility on the logical network paths within the schedule model.
Expansion: Critical path method is a schedule analysis method that calculates the longest path of dependent activities through a project network and identifies the shortest possible duration for the project. PMI uses it to determine which activities have zero or very little total float, making them the primary drivers of finish-date performance. It is central to predictive scheduling and remains useful even when projects use rolling wave planning or mixed delivery approaches. The method supports schedule compression, forecasting, and impact analysis when delays or changes occur.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Critical path method, often shortened to CPM, analyses activity durations, dependencies, and network logic to find the path that governs the project's earliest possible completion. Activities on the critical path cannot slip without affecting the planned finish date unless corrective action is taken elsewhere.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps project managers focus attention on the work that most directly controls delivery timing. It is most visible in predictive schedules for construction, engineering, infrastructure, product launches, and other projects where sequence logic and finish-date commitments matter.

The most common misconception: that the critical path is simply the list of longest-duration activities or that it never changes. In practice it is determined by network logic, constraints, leads and lags, and actual progress, so the critical path can shift during execution.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use CPM to build baseline schedules, identify near-critical work, test what-if scenarios, and decide whether crashing or fast tracking is needed after a delay to preserve milestone dates.
Contrast: Critical chain method — CPM focuses on the longest logical path and float in the schedule network; critical chain method also accounts for resource constraints and uses buffers.
Example: On a hospital expansion project, excavation, foundation, steel erection, and inspection activities form the critical path, so a delay in steel delivery pushes the planned opening date unless the team compresses later work.

Core Meaning: The point in time through which schedule progress information is known and statused.
Expansion: Data date is the cutoff date used when updating a schedule model with actual progress. It separates completed or statused work from future planned work in forecasting calculations. In scheduling practice, it anchors remaining duration, critical path analysis, and variance interpretation after a status update.
Contrast: Status date — Data date is the formal scheduling cutoff used in the model; status date is a broader reporting term that may be used more loosely.
Example: A construction scheduler updates the programme as of 30 June and uses that data date to recalculate forecast finish dates for all remaining activities.
Source: Practice Standard for Scheduling, Glossary, data date, p. 183

Core Meaning: The process of identifying and documenting the specific actions to be performed to produce the project deliverables.
Expansion: Define Activities breaks work packages and planning components into the discrete activities needed to create the planned outputs. It makes the schedule actionable by identifying what work must actually be performed rather than stopping at high-level scope statements. This process creates the activity list and related attributes used in sequencing, estimating, and scheduling.
Contrast: Create WBS — Create WBS decomposes scope into deliverables and work packages; Define Activities identifies the actions required to produce those deliverables.
Example: For an office relocation project, the team defines activities such as survey the new site, procure furniture, install network cabling, move equipment, and complete occupancy testing.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition — Define Activities, page 183

Core Meaning: A condition in which planned work or progress occurs later than scheduled, affecting activity timing, milestones, or overall project completion.
Expansion: In project management, delay refers to slippage against the planned schedule at the activity, milestone, or project level. It may result from late decisions, resource shortages, rework, external dependencies, or unforeseen events.
Contrast: Lag — delay is an outcome or condition of lateness in the schedule; lag is an intentionally modeled waiting period between activities.
Example: A permitting approval arrives three weeks late, delaying site mobilisation and pushing the construction start milestone beyond the baseline date.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A relationship in which one activity, task, or outcome relies on another for sequencing, input, or completion.
Expansion: A dependency expresses how work is connected so that one element cannot start, finish, or proceed as intended without another element occurring first or in a defined order. In project management, dependencies are critical for schedule logic, coordination across teams, and identifying constraints that affect delivery. They may be mandatory, discretionary, internal, or external depending on the nature of the relationship.
Contrast: Predecessor — a dependency is the relationship itself; a predecessor is the specific activity that comes before another in that relationship.
Example: A testing activity depends on completion of software configuration because the test team needs a stable build before execution can begin.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — Dependency

Core Meaning: The act of identifying and defining logical relationships among project activities.
Expansion: Dependency determination establishes how activities relate to one another so that sequencing reflects real technical, external, and discretionary constraints. In PMI practice, these relationships may include mandatory, discretionary, internal, and external dependencies and are essential for building a valid schedule model. Clear dependency determination improves the realism of critical path analysis, float calculations, and schedule forecasts.
Contrast: Sequence Activities — dependency determination identifies the logical relationships themselves; Sequence Activities is the broader process that uses those relationships to order the work.
Example: When planning a data-center migration, the team determines that server configuration must finish before cutover testing can begin, while user training can run partly in parallel.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition — Sequence Activities, page 185

Core Meaning: The identification, analysis, sequencing, monitoring, and coordination of relationships in which one activity, deliverable, team, or outcome relies on another.
Expansion: Dependency management helps a project understand how work items influence one another so that plans, commitments, and handoffs remain realistic. In PMI-aligned practice, it supports scheduling, risk management, and cross-team coordination by making constraints and interfaces visible. It is particularly important in complex projects, programs, and product environments where delays or changes in one area can cascade into others.
Contrast: Constraint — a dependency is a reliance relationship between work elements; a constraint is a limiting factor such as time, budget, or policy.
Example: A system-testing phase cannot begin until the data-migration team completes environment preparation, so the project manager tracks that dependency and escalates slippage early.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Identifying and visualizing the links between activities, teams, or deliverables so ordering and hand-off risks are managed early.
Expansion: Dependency mapping makes explicit which pieces of work rely on others — within a team, across teams, or on external parties. Captured as a diagram or matrix, it exposes sequencing constraints, critical hand-offs, and single points of failure early, so the schedule and integration can be planned around them. It is especially valuable at program scale and in large planning events, where cross-team dependencies are a common source of delay.
Contrast: Precedence diagram (activity network) — dependency mapping is the broader practice of surfacing all dependencies including cross-team and external ones, while a precedence diagram is the formal activity-on-node network used to sequence a single schedule.
Example: At the planning event the teams draw dependency lines between features and find two teams both need the shared component in the same iteration, so they re-sequence to remove the bottleneck.
Source: Scaled-agile / schedule practice — dependency mapping

Core Meaning: The process of analyzing activity sequences, durations, resource requirements, and schedule constraints to create the project schedule model for execution and control.
Expansion: Develop Schedule turns activity and resource planning data into a workable schedule model. It evaluates sequencing logic, durations, calendars, constraints, and resource availability to forecast start and finish dates. The result supports execution, progress tracking, forecasting, and schedule control throughout the project.
Contrast: Define Activities — Define Activities identifies the work to be scheduled; Develop Schedule analyzes that work to produce the schedule model.
Example: A construction manager sequences excavation, concrete, framing, and inspection activities to produce the approved project schedule.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Develop Schedule, page 715

Core Meaning: A relationship that is based on preferred sequencing of activities and is not required by the nature of the work.
Expansion: A discretionary dependency reflects a chosen order of work, often based on best practice, experience, or a preferred approach. Unlike mandatory dependencies, it can be changed if the team decides a different sequence is better. PMI treats it as a scheduling choice that should be documented because it affects logic, float, and schedule flexibility.
Contrast: Mandatory dependency — a discretionary dependency is chosen by the team; a mandatory dependency is required by the work, contract, or physical reality.
Example: A team chooses to complete user interface design before backend integration even though the two could be developed in parallel.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Discretionary dependency, page 716

Core Meaning: A schedule activity with zero duration used to show a logical relationship in an activity-on-arrow network diagram.
Expansion: A dummy activity does not represent real work. It is inserted only to preserve correct network logic when two activities need to be distinguished in the diagram. PMI uses it in activity-on-arrow scheduling to maintain accurate precedence relationships without adding duration or resources.
Contrast: Real activity — a dummy activity is only a logic placeholder; a real activity represents actual work that consumes time and resources.
Example: In a network diagram, a dummy activity is added so two successor tasks can share a predecessor relationship without implying extra work.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Dummy activity, page 716

Core Meaning: The total number of work periods needed to complete an activity, phase, or project.
Expansion: Duration is the elapsed working time required for a scheduled activity or component, taking into account the planned resource availability and working calendar. It differs from effort because it reflects how long the work takes on the schedule, not simply how much labor is expended. Duration is a basic input to schedule development, forecasting, and critical path analysis.
Contrast: Effort — duration is elapsed time on the schedule; effort is the amount of labor or work content required.
Example: A task estimated at 40 hours of effort may have a duration of 5 working days if one full-time analyst is assigned, or 3 days if two analysts split the work with some overlap.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / PMI Lexicon — Duration

Core Meaning: A schedule-shortening approach used to reduce the project or activity timeline without changing scope.
Expansion: Duration compression is the deliberate use of techniques such as crashing and fast tracking to shorten the schedule while keeping the agreed scope intact. PMI treats it as a schedule optimisation response when deadlines tighten or delays threaten key milestones. It is useful when time is the primary constraint, but it often increases cost, coordination complexity, or risk.
Deep Dive:
Duration compression is a way to reduce planned completion time by reworking how and when activities are performed, without changing scope. There are two main techniques:

- Crashing — adding resources to critical-path work to finish it faster, usually at added cost.
- Fast tracking — overlapping activities that were originally planned in sequence, usually at added risk.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects often face immovable launch dates, regulatory deadlines, contract commitments, or recovery needs after slippage. Duration compression gives the project manager structured options to regain time while preserving scope, especially in schedule-driven projects such as product launches, construction phases, and system deployments.

The most common misconception is that schedule compression is simply working harder or asking everyone to move faster. In practice, effective compression is a selective analysis of critical-path options, trade-offs, and added risk, not a blanket demand for urgency.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team may assign additional skilled resources to a critical integration task, or begin user training before all configuration work is fully complete where the overlap is manageable. Each move is evaluated for downstream risk, cost impact, and whether the time saved is real rather than apparent.
Contrast: Schedule compression — duration compression is the specific reduction of elapsed time; schedule compression is the broader family of techniques used to shorten the schedule.
Example: A software rollout is behind plan, so the team adds an extra integration specialist to the critical path and overlaps training-material development with final configuration review to recover two weeks.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Develop Schedule process, schedule compression techniques; confirm exact page in local edition

Core Meaning: An assessment of the likely amount of time needed to complete an activity or work package.
Expansion: A duration estimate expresses how long work is expected to take, considering resources, productivity, risks, and relevant constraints. It is used in schedule development and may be stated as a point estimate or a range. Good duration estimates support realistic sequencing, forecasting, and schedule commitments.
Contrast: Effort estimate — duration is elapsed working time to complete the work; effort is the amount of labor required, which may differ depending on resource allocation.
Example: A testing activity is estimated at five working days because two testers can execute the planned test set within that time under current environment constraints.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Estimate Activity Durations process; confirm exact page in local edition

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can finish based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: Early Finish is calculated during schedule network analysis and shows the soonest an activity could complete if predecessor work occurs as planned. It is typically derived from the Early Start plus the activity duration. PMI uses it to support forward-pass calculations, float analysis, and schedule feasibility checks.
Contrast: Late Finish — Early Finish is the earliest feasible completion point, while Late Finish is the latest completion point that avoids delaying the project or successor work.
Example: If design is scheduled to start on Monday and takes five working days, the design activity may have an Early Finish of Friday.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) glossary, Early Finish, p. 243

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can finish based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: The early finish date is calculated during schedule network analysis to show when an activity could complete if preceding work occurs as planned. It is typically derived from the early start date plus the activity duration, adjusted for the scheduling method in use. It matters because it helps identify the earliest project timeline and supports float analysis. Project teams use it to understand schedule feasibility before resource or calendar constraints are fully applied.
Contrast: Late finish date — early finish shows the earliest feasible completion point, while late finish shows the latest completion point without delaying the project or successor work.
Example: In a construction schedule, foundation curing has an early finish date of 14 May if excavation and pouring both complete on time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, early finish date, page 243

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can begin based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: Early Start is the earliest time an activity can begin when predecessor relationships and durations are applied in schedule analysis. It is produced through the forward pass and is a key input to Early Finish calculations. PMI uses it to understand sequencing effects and the earliest achievable schedule.
Contrast: Late Start — Early Start is the earliest feasible beginning point, while Late Start is the latest start that avoids delaying the project or successor work.
Example: After approval is planned to finish on 3 March, procurement can have an Early Start of 4 March.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) glossary, Early Start, p. 243

Core Meaning: The earliest possible point in time at which a schedule activity can begin based on the network logic and durations.
Expansion: The early start date is the earliest time an activity can begin when predecessor relationships and assumed durations are applied in schedule analysis. It is produced through a forward pass in network calculations and forms the basis for the earliest achievable schedule. Teams use it to understand sequencing effects and potential schedule flexibility. It is a core input to determining early finish dates and float.
Contrast: Late start date — early start shows the earliest feasible beginning point, while late start shows the latest start that avoids delaying the project or successor work.
Example: After design approval is planned to finish on 3 March, procurement receives an early start date of 4 March in the project schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, early start date, page 243

Core Meaning: An extension of earned value that measures schedule performance in time units rather than cost.
Expansion: Earned schedule refines earned value management by expressing schedule progress in terms of time. It finds the point in time at which the planned value should have equaled the current earned value, producing schedule indicators that stay meaningful late in a project. It addresses a known weakness of traditional schedule variance, which is measured in cost and loses accuracy as a project nears completion.
Contrast: Schedule variance - traditional schedule variance is measured in cost units and distorts near project end, whereas earned schedule measures performance in time units and remains meaningful.
Example: Instead of reporting schedule variance in dollars, a PM uses earned schedule to report that the project is running two weeks behind.
Source: Earned schedule method (extension of EVM, Practice Standard for Earned Value Management)

Core Meaning: The number of labor units required to complete a schedule activity or work breakdown structure component, often expressed in hours, days, or weeks.
Expansion: Effort expresses how much human work is needed to complete a defined piece of work. It supports estimating, staffing, scheduling, and capacity decisions, but it is not the same as elapsed duration because resource availability and sequencing affect calendar time. In practice, teams use effort estimates to size work packages and assign people with the right skills. Clear effort estimates improve forecast quality and workload balancing.
Contrast: Duration — effort is the labor required to do the work, while duration is the total elapsed calendar time needed to finish it.
Example: A business analyst estimates that documenting requirements will take 40 hours of effort, which may span two calendar weeks if the analyst is only assigned part time.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Effort; page citation not confirmed in available source

Core Meaning: The total calendar time between the start and finish of an activity, including non-working periods.
Expansion: Elapsed time measures duration on the calendar from start to end, counting weekends, holidays, and idle waiting alongside actual working time. It differs from effort, which counts only the labor expended, so a task needing two hours of work can have an elapsed time of several days if it waits in a queue.
Contrast: Effort is the work performed, while elapsed time is the wall-clock span over which it happens.
Example: A lab test requiring ten minutes of work has an elapsed time of three days because results take that long to return.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms

Core Meaning: Estimate Activity Durations is the process of approximating the number of work periods needed to complete individual activities with estimated resources.
Expansion: This schedule management process develops duration estimates for the activities identified in the project schedule model. PMI positions it after activities are defined and sequenced because duration estimates depend on activity scope, resource capability, calendars, and constraints. Its output supports schedule development, forecasting, and analysis of delivery feasibility.
Contrast: Estimate Costs — Estimate Activity Durations approximates the time needed to complete activities, while Estimate Costs approximates the monetary resources required for project work.
Example: On a software project, the team estimates that configuring identity-management integration will take eight working days using one architect and two developers.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Sixth Edition, 6.4 Estimate Activity Durations, p. 200

Core Meaning: A relationship between a project activity and a non-project activity that affects timing or execution.
Expansion: An external dependency exists when project work depends on something outside the direct control of the project team. This may involve regulators, vendors, customers, operational groups, or other external activities. PMI treats it as a scheduling and coordination consideration because it can constrain sequencing, introduce risk, and affect delivery commitments.
Contrast: Internal dependency — an external dependency relies on activities outside the project boundary or team control; an internal dependency exists between activities within the project.
Example: A construction project cannot begin site excavation until a city agency issues the required permit, creating an external dependency on the regulator.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — External dependencies

Core Meaning: A schedule compression technique in which activities or phases normally done in sequence are performed in parallel for at least part of their duration.
Expansion: Fast tracking shortens the schedule by overlapping work that was originally planned sequentially. It can reduce overall project duration without immediately adding resources, but it often increases coordination complexity and the risk of rework. PMI treats it as a schedule compression technique used when time pressure outweighs the added delivery risk.
Contrast: Crashing — fast tracking shortens duration by overlapping activities, while crashing shortens duration by adding resources.
Example: A construction project starts interior fit-out work before all exterior work is fully complete so the overall completion date can be pulled forward.
Source: PMI Lexicon / PMBOK Guide glossary, Fast tracking; page citation not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: The point in time associated with the completion of a schedule activity.
Expansion: Finish Date identifies when a scheduled activity is planned or calculated to be complete. It is used in schedule development and control alongside related terms such as early finish and late finish. Finish dates help teams understand sequencing, monitor progress, and evaluate the effect of delays on downstream work.
Contrast: Early Finish — Finish Date is the general completion point for an activity, while Early Finish is the earliest possible completion date based on schedule logic.
Example: A testing activity is planned to finish on 18 July, and that date is recorded as its finish date in the schedule.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish date, page 181

Core Meaning: A schedule constraint that sets the earliest allowable finish date for an activity.
Expansion: Finish not earlier than prevents an activity from finishing before a specified date. It is used when external commitments, approvals, or timing windows require work to end no sooner than a given point in time.
Contrast: Finish not later than — Finish not earlier than sets the earliest allowed finish, while Finish not later than sets the latest allowed finish.
Example: A regulatory review must not finish before the public comment period closes, so the activity is constrained with finish not earlier than.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish not earlier than, page 181

Core Meaning: A schedule constraint that sets the latest allowable finish date for an activity.
Expansion: Finish not later than prevents an activity from finishing after a specified date. It is used when a deadline, milestone, or external dependency requires completion by a fixed point in time.
Contrast: Finish not earlier than — Finish not later than sets the latest allowed finish, while Finish not earlier than sets the earliest allowed finish.
Example: A contract deliverable must be completed by the end of the quarter, so the schedule applies a finish not later than constraint.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish not later than, page 181

Core Meaning: A schedule constraint that requires an activity to finish on a specified date.
Expansion: Finish on fixes the completion date of an activity to a specific point in time. It is used when the finish date is externally imposed and cannot move without violating the schedule requirement.
Contrast: Finish not later than — Finish on requires completion on one exact date, while Finish not later than allows completion on or before a date.
Example: A project milestone tied to a product launch is constrained to finish on the launch date.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish on, page 181

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-Finish is a schedule dependency that links the completion of one activity to the completion of another. It does not require the two activities to start together, only that the successor cannot finish before the predecessor finishes. PMI includes it as one of the four core precedence relationships used in schedule network logic.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Finish-to-Finish controls when the successor can finish, while Finish-to-Start controls when the successor can start.
Example: Final proofreading cannot finish until final layout updates are complete, so the two activities are linked with a Finish-to-Finish relationship.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish-to-Finish, page 181

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-Finish is one of the precedence relationships used in schedule network analysis. It links the completion of one activity to the completion of another rather than linking their start dates. Project managers use it to model work that may proceed in parallel but must conclude in a defined sequence.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Finish-to-Finish controls when the successor can finish; Finish-to-Start controls when the successor can start.
Example: Quality review can run while document drafting is underway, but the review activity cannot finish until drafting is complete, so the tasks are linked Finish-to-Finish.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Finish-to-Finish relationship, p. 127

Core Meaning: A precedence relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until its predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-start is the most common logical relationship used in project schedule network diagrams. It defines sequence by making the start of one activity dependent on the completion of another. Project teams use it to build realistic schedules, analyse dependencies, and evaluate the effects of delays across the network.
Contrast: Start-to-start — finish-to-start requires the predecessor to finish before the successor starts, while start-to-start only requires the predecessor to start before the successor can start.
Example: In a building project, final electrical inspection must finish before the occupancy permit application can start.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, schedule network relationship definitions, exact page citation to confirm in PMI source

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until a predecessor activity has finished.
Expansion: Finish-to-Start is the most common dependency used in project schedules. It defines a handoff in which one piece of work must be completed before the next can begin. PMI treats it as one of the core logical relationship types used to build schedule networks and analyse project timing.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Finish-to-Start is a dependency between two activities where the predecessor must finish before the successor can start. It is the default sequencing logic used in many schedules because it reflects straightforward task handoffs.

Why it matters and where it applies: it allows teams to model real execution order, calculate the critical path, and forecast how delays will affect later work. It is widely used in construction, systems implementation, product delivery, and any project where one output enables the next step.

The most common misconception: that every dependency should be Finish-to-Start. Some work is better represented with Start-to-Start or Finish-to-Finish relationships, and forcing everything into Finish-to-Start logic can distort the schedule.

How it shows up in real projects: approval must finish before procurement can start, code development must finish before formal system testing begins, or excavation must finish before foundation pouring starts.
Contrast: Start-to-Start relationship — Finish-to-Start requires the predecessor to finish before the successor starts, while Start-to-Start requires only that the predecessor start before the successor starts.
Example: A data migration activity cannot start until the data cleansing activity has finished.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Finish-to-Start, page 117; glossary index page 181

Core Meaning: A scheduling constraint or condition in which the activity duration remains set and changes are managed by adjusting resources or scope rather than elapsed time.
Expansion: Fixed duration means the planned time span for the work is held constant. If performance issues arise, the team usually changes staffing, productivity assumptions, or work content instead of extending the duration.
Contrast: Fixed units — fixed duration holds time constant, while fixed units holds the assigned resource quantity constant.
Example: A five-day user training event must occur within one workweek, so the team adds facilitators rather than extending the session length.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A type of activity in which the number of resource units assigned remains constant regardless of the amount of work or duration.
Expansion: Fixed units means the resource allocation stays constant even if the activity duration or work changes. This is useful when a specific staffing level or equipment count must be maintained for the activity. PMI uses it as one of the activity type settings that influence how schedule calculations respond to changes.
Contrast: Fixed work — Fixed units keeps the resource units constant, while fixed work keeps the total effort constant.
Example: A concrete pour is planned with two crane operators throughout the activity, so the resource units remain fixed even if the duration shifts.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A type of activity in which the amount of work remains constant regardless of the number of resources assigned.
Expansion: Fixed work means the total effort required for the activity does not change when resources are added or removed. If more people are assigned, the duration may decrease, but the work itself stays the same. PMI uses this concept when discussing activity types and how resource assignment affects schedule calculations.
Contrast: Fixed duration — Fixed work keeps the effort constant, while fixed duration keeps the time constant regardless of resource assignment.
Example: A 40-hour requirements review remains a 40-hour effort whether one analyst or two analysts are assigned, although the elapsed duration may change.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The amount of time that a schedule activity can be delayed without delaying the project finish date or a related schedule constraint, depending on the type of float being used.
Expansion: Float, also called slack, indicates scheduling flexibility within a project network. PMI uses the term broadly and distinguishes among forms such as free float and total float. Understanding float helps project managers identify where delay is tolerable, where schedule risk is concentrated, and which activities are likely to be critical.
Contrast: Total Float — Float is the general concept of scheduling flexibility, while Total Float is the specific amount an activity can slip without delaying the project end date or violating a schedule constraint.
Example: A reporting task has three days of float, so it can move later within that window without affecting the planned project completion date.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Float, page 181

Core Meaning: A Critical Path Method technique for calculating the early start and early finish dates by working forward through the schedule model from the project start date or a given point in time.
Expansion: Forward Pass is the schedule calculation used to determine the earliest dates activities can begin and end based on durations and dependencies. It moves from the start of the network toward the finish and establishes the earliest possible project completion date under the current logic. PMI treats it as a core element of schedule network analysis and critical path work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Forward Pass is a step-by-step calculation through the schedule network that derives early start and early finish dates for each activity. It uses predecessor logic and activity durations to show the earliest feasible timing of the work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is essential for schedule development, critical path analysis, and evaluating whether target dates are achievable. It is commonly used in predictive planning where teams need a logic-driven view of timing before adding resource or calendar constraints.

The most common misconception: that forward pass produces the final committed schedule dates. It only shows the earliest possible dates based on logic and duration, and those dates may later change because of constraints, resource limits, or management decisions.

How it shows up in real projects: planners calculate when design can finish, procurement can start, testing can begin, and the project could complete at the earliest if each predecessor finishes as modelled.
Contrast: Backward Pass — Forward Pass calculates earliest possible dates from the start of the schedule, while Backward Pass calculates latest allowable dates from the end.
Example: A project planner runs a forward pass through the activity network to find the earliest date system integration testing can begin after build and configuration tasks are complete.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Forward Pass, page 181

Core Meaning: The amount of time a schedule activity can be delayed without delaying the early start date of any successor or violating a schedule constraint.
Expansion: Free Float measures how much scheduling flexibility an individual activity has before its delay affects the next activity in the network. It is narrower than total float because it focuses on immediate successors rather than the project completion date. PMI uses it in schedule analysis to understand local flexibility and spot potential slippage before it propagates.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Free Float is the amount of delay an activity can absorb without pushing the early start of any successor activity. It is a property of the network logic around that activity, not a general permission to slip work without consequence.

Why it matters and where it applies: it helps project managers see which tasks have limited local flexibility and which can move slightly without disrupting follow-on work. This is useful in detailed schedule control, short-interval planning, and coordination-heavy projects where successor timing matters.

The most common misconception: that Free Float and Total Float are the same. Free Float is more restrictive because an activity may have some room before affecting the overall project finish date but still have no room before affecting its immediate successor.

How it shows up in real projects: a team sees that a configuration task can slip one day without delaying system testing, but any further delay will move the testing start and create a visible downstream impact.
Contrast: Total Float — Free Float protects the early start of successor activities, while Total Float protects the project finish date or a schedule constraint.
Example: A design review can slip by two days without delaying the start of procurement, so the review activity has two days of free float.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Free Float, page 181

Core Meaning: A bar chart of schedule information where activities are listed on the vertical axis, dates are shown on the horizontal axis, and activity durations are shown as horizontal bars placed according to start and finish dates.
Expansion: A Gantt chart is a common schedule visualisation used to show planned and actual timing for project activities. It helps teams and stakeholders see sequencing, overlaps, milestones, and progress at a glance. PMI includes it as a practical way to communicate schedule information, especially in predictive or hybrid planning environments.
Contrast: Network diagram — a Gantt chart emphasises dates and durations on a timeline; a network diagram emphasises logical relationships and dependencies between activities.
Example: A construction manager uses a Gantt chart to show excavation, foundation, framing, and inspection dates across the project timeline.
