Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Gantt chart

Core Meaning: A summary activity that spans a segment of a project schedule and derives its duration from the activities it covers.
Expansion: A hammock activity is a grouping activity used to summarize part of the schedule rather than represent distinct work. Its start and finish are driven by the earliest and latest dates of the detailed activities within its span. It is useful for reporting, oversight, and communicating schedule windows at a higher level without replacing the detailed network logic underneath.
Contrast: Summary milestone — a hammock activity spans time and derives duration from underlying work; a milestone is a zero-duration point in time.
Example: In a construction project, 'Site preparation oversight' is shown as a hammock activity spanning excavation, grading, and utility trenching so leadership can track that phase as one schedule band.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide, page 181

Core Meaning: A logical relationship between activities that is required by the nature of the work or by contractual or physical constraints.
Expansion: Hard logic refers to dependencies that cannot be changed without violating how the work must actually occur. In scheduling practice, these relationships are driven by mandatory technical, legal, safety, or physical conditions rather than team preference. PMI commonly treats this idea under mandatory dependency rather than as a separately emphasised glossary term.
Contrast: Soft logic — hard logic is mandatory because of the work's inherent constraints; soft logic is discretionary and based on preferred sequencing.
Example: On a construction project, the concrete foundation must cure before structural steel can be erected on it.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Estimating effort or duration using experience-based rules of thumb rather than detailed analysis.
Expansion: Heuristic estimation applies a generally accepted rule of thumb learned from past projects to size new work quickly, for example assuming testing takes roughly 20 percent of coding time. It trades precision for speed and is most useful early on or when detailed data is unavailable.
Contrast: Parametric estimating - parametric estimating uses a statistical relationship between historical data and project variables, whereas heuristic estimating relies on experience-based rules of thumb.
Example: A manager estimates a new module at 15 percent of total project effort based on a long-standing team rule of thumb.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An estimate of how long work would take with no interruptions and all needed resources immediately available.
Expansion: Ideal time, also called ideal days or ideal hours, is the effort an item would require under perfect conditions, with no distractions, meetings, or waiting. Because real workdays carry overhead, ideal time is always less than elapsed calendar time, so teams apply a load factor when forecasting actual delivery.
Contrast: Elapsed time - elapsed time is the actual calendar time a task takes including interruptions, whereas ideal time assumes perfect, uninterrupted conditions.
Example: A developer estimates a story at three ideal days, knowing it will span roughly a full calendar week once meetings and context-switching are counted.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A fixed calendar date forced onto an activity or milestone that constrains when it can start or finish.
Expansion: An imposed date is an externally dictated constraint, typically expressed as 'start no earlier than' or 'finish no later than', that limits the scheduling flexibility of an activity or milestone. Such dates often stem from contracts, regulations, market windows, or weather, and the scheduling tool must honor them during network analysis.
Contrast: Milestone - a milestone marks a significant point or event in the schedule, whereas an imposed date is a constraint fixing when something must occur.
Example: A regulatory submission must 'finish no later than' June 30, an imposed date that drives the project's backward pass.
Source: PMBOK Guide - imposed date

Core Meaning: A dependency between project activities or elements that are within the control or scope of the project team.
Expansion: An internal dependency exists when one project activity depends on another activity inside the same project. PMI uses the distinction to separate relationships the team can largely manage from those driven by outside parties or conditions. This helps with sequencing work, assigning accountability, and building realistic schedules.
Contrast: External dependency — an internal dependency is within the project team's work or control; an external dependency relies on parties or events outside the project.
Example: System testing depends on code completion by the development team, so the testing start date is an internal dependency in the project schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), dependency classifications including internal and external dependencies, p. 215

Core Meaning: The amount of time whereby a successor activity is delayed with respect to a predecessor activity.
Expansion: Lag is a schedule offset that intentionally delays a successor activity after its predecessor. PMI treats it as part of activity relationship modeling in schedule network analysis. It is useful when the work includes real waiting time such as curing, approval, or settling periods.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Lag is a time delay built into the logical relationship between two activities. It changes when the successor can begin or finish relative to the predecessor.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because many projects include necessary waiting periods that are part of the work logic, not accidental delay. It appears often in construction, engineering, procurement, validation, and any schedule that must reflect real-world timing constraints.

The most common misconception: Lag is often treated as harmless idle time or a convenient shortcut in scheduling software. In practice, too much hidden lag can obscure risk, reduce transparency, and make the schedule harder to manage.

How it shows up in real projects: A construction team pours concrete, then inserts a curing delay before structural loading can begin. A regulated product team submits documentation, then waits through a review period before the next controlled step can start.
Contrast: Lead — lag delays the successor activity; lead allows the successor to start earlier.
Example: A site schedule includes a 5 day lag after painting so inspection starts only after the coating has fully cured.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, glossary, p. 182

Core Meaning: The latest possible point in time when the uncompleted portions of a schedule activity can finish without delaying the project finish date.
Expansion: Late Finish is a core schedule-network calculation used in critical path method analysis. It expresses the latest allowable finish for an activity based on network logic, successor relationships, and project or milestone constraints. Project teams use it to determine schedule flexibility and calculate total float. It is fundamental to understanding which activities can slip and which directly threaten completion dates.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Late Finish is one of the basic time-analysis values in network scheduling. It is calculated during the backward pass and shows the latest date an activity can finish without pushing the required project or milestone completion.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is essential in critical path method analysis, float calculation, and schedule risk interpretation. Teams use it when evaluating sequencing options, understanding schedule sensitivity, and identifying activities with little or no room for delay.

The most common misconception: that Late Finish is a target finish date or a recommended finish date. It is neither; it is a calculated limit based on the network logic and constraints, and finishing later than it indicates delay to a dependent milestone or the overall project.

How it shows up in real projects: schedulers compare an activity's forecast finish against its Late Finish to see whether available float is being consumed. In status reviews, activities near or beyond their Late Finish usually trigger escalation, resequencing, or recovery planning.
Contrast: Early Finish — Late Finish is the latest allowable finish without delaying the project or milestone, while Early Finish is the earliest possible finish based on current logic.
Example: A testing activity has a Late Finish of Friday because any later completion would push the release milestone and consume all remaining float.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Late Finish, page 717

Core Meaning: In the critical path method, the latest possible point in time when the uncompleted portions of a schedule activity can finish based on the schedule network logic, the project completion date, and any schedule constraints.
Expansion: Late finish date is a schedule calculation used to determine the latest time an activity can complete without delaying the project finish. It is derived from the network logic and any imposed constraints. PMI uses it in critical path method analysis alongside early finish date and late start date.
Contrast: Early finish date — late finish date is the latest allowable finish without affecting the project end; early finish date is the earliest possible finish.
Example: A testing activity has a late finish date of Friday because any later completion would push the release milestone.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Late Finish Date

Core Meaning: In the critical path method, the latest possible point in time when the uncompleted portions of a schedule activity can start based on the schedule network logic, the project completion date, and any schedule constraints.
Expansion: Late start date shows the latest time an activity may begin without delaying the project finish. It is calculated from the schedule network logic and project completion date, with any constraints applied. PMI lists it with the other critical path timing dates used in schedule analysis.
Contrast: Early start date — late start date is the latest allowable start without affecting the project end; early start date is the earliest possible start.
Example: A procurement task must start no later than Tuesday to keep the equipment delivery aligned with installation.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Late Start Date

Core Meaning: The amount of time whereby a successor activity can be advanced with respect to a predecessor activity.
Expansion: Lead is a schedule offset that allows a successor activity to begin before its predecessor has fully finished. PMI treats it as a way to model overlap between related activities in a schedule network. It is useful for shortening duration when partial information or partial completion is enough to start downstream work.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Lead is a time adjustment in a logical relationship that advances a successor activity. It creates intentional overlap between activities that would otherwise occur in a stricter sequence.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because teams often compress schedules by starting downstream work as soon as enough upstream output is available. It is common in design, software, construction, testing, and other environments where partial completion can support an early start.

The most common misconception: Lead is often assumed to be a free acceleration with no downside. In reality, overlapping work increases coordination demands and can create rework if the predecessor changes.

How it shows up in real projects: A software team begins integration testing before every module is finished. A design team starts procurement on approved long lead items while final details for other components are still being completed.
Contrast: Lag — lead advances the successor activity; lag delays it.
Example: An engineering project starts procurement of standard equipment 10 days before the full design package is finalized.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, glossary, p. 182

Core Meaning: Schedule adjustments that advance or delay the timing between logically related activities.
Expansion: Lead and lag are timing modifiers applied to activity relationships in a project schedule. A lead lets successor work begin earlier than the basic relationship would otherwise allow, while a lag inserts waiting time before successor work can proceed. PMI treats them as standard schedule-network concepts used to model overlap and delay realistically. They are foundational because they directly affect sequencing, forecasting, and critical-path behaviour.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Lead and lag are relationship-based schedule offsets. They do not replace dependencies; they refine how those dependencies behave in time.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects rarely move in perfectly clean finish-to-start handoffs. Construction, engineering, procurement, testing, and approvals often require overlap or waiting periods, and lead and lag let the schedule represent those conditions more accurately.

The most common misconception: that lead and lag are harmless software shortcuts. In reality, overuse can hide true work logic, reduce schedule transparency, and distort analysis if teams use them instead of modelling the actual activities.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may apply a lag after concrete placement to reflect curing time before inspection, or apply a lead so equipment setup can start shortly before all design documentation is fully complete.
Contrast: Dependency — lead and lag adjust the timing within a dependency; dependency is the underlying logical relationship itself.
Example: After applying floor coating, a facilities project adds a two-day lag before occupancy testing, while furniture installation starts with a one-day lead before punch-list completion.
Source: Practice Standard for Scheduling / PMI Lexicon

Core Meaning: A scheduling approach that applies Lean principles to improve flow and reduce waste in the schedule.
Expansion: Lean scheduling uses Lean thinking to create schedules that support smoother flow of work and fewer delays. The knowledgebase index shows it as a distinct term associated with scheduling. It is used when the goal is to improve reliability and reduce waiting rather than only to sequence activities.
Contrast: Critical path scheduling — Lean scheduling emphasizes flow and waste reduction; critical path scheduling emphasizes the longest path that determines project duration.
Example: A construction team sequences trades to reduce idle time between crews and avoid unnecessary waiting on site.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase / Lean scheduling

Core Meaning: An activity that does not produce definitive end products and is measured by the passage of time.
Expansion: Level of effort activities support the project but do not create discrete deliverables that can be measured by output. PMI distinguishes them from discrete effort and apportioned effort. They are typically ongoing support or oversight work whose value is tied to duration rather than a finished product.
Contrast: Discrete effort — level of effort is measured by time spent; discrete effort is measured by completion of specific deliverables.
Example: Project management oversight is tracked as level of effort because it continues throughout the project rather than ending with a single deliverable.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Level of Effort

Core Meaning: A scheduling and control technique for tracking planned and actual progress across repetitive units, locations, or production segments.
Expansion: Line of balance is a planning and control technique used when similar work repeats across multiple units or areas. It focuses on production flow and the rate at which crews or work packages move through repeated scopes. It is especially useful in construction, infrastructure, housing, and other environments where the same sequence of work recurs many times.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Line of balance is a visual and analytical technique for managing repetitive work. Instead of focusing only on one network of unique activities, it compares the planned pace of work against actual progress across repeated locations, units, or sections.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because repetitive projects succeed or fail based on flow, crew continuity, and production rhythm. It is particularly useful in highways, pipelines, rail, high-rise floors, housing developments, and any project where similar tasks repeat in sequence across many work fronts.

The most common misconception: that line of balance is just another Gantt chart. In practice, it is more about production rate and work continuity than simple task timing, and it helps reveal crew clashes, waiting, and loss of flow that a standard bar chart may hide.

How it shows up in real projects: A contractor uses line of balance to track framing, electrical, and finishing crews across apartment floors so each trade moves steadily without overtaking or blocking the next. A road project uses it to monitor paving operations section by section and spot where production is slipping behind the planned rhythm.
Contrast: Critical path method — line of balance manages repetitive flow-based work; critical path method manages dependency-based sequences across varied activities.
Example: A housing development team tracks plumbing, electrical, and drywall crews across dozens of near-identical units to keep each trade moving at a stable pace without trade interference.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A scheduling approach that organises and sequences work by physical location to manage production flow across repeated or segmented work areas.
Expansion: Location-based scheduling plans work around where activities occur, not only around activity lists and precedence logic. In PMI practice, it is valuable where crews move through multiple locations in a repeatable pattern, such as floors, zones, road segments, or units. It helps coordinate handoffs between trades, reduce congestion, and stabilise workflow. The concept is especially relevant in construction and other production-like project environments.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Location-based scheduling arranges work by location and by the flow of crews or work packages from one location to the next. Location becomes a primary organising factor in the schedule logic.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters in projects with repeated work across multiple sites or zones, where smooth crew movement and trade coordination affect productivity. It is commonly used in buildings, transportation, utilities, and other physical delivery environments.

The most common misconception: that it is merely a different way to display a schedule. In practice it is a planning and control method that uses location and flow to shape sequencing decisions.

How it shows up in real projects: teams schedule drywall, electrical, and finishing crews by floor or zone so each trade can progress steadily without blocking the next.
Contrast: Network schedule — location-based scheduling is organised around physical location and flow, while a network schedule is organised around activity dependencies.
Example: A high-rise project sequences interior trades floor by floor so crews can move in a controlled production line with fewer clashes.
Source: Construction Extension to the PMBOK Guide / PMI Lexicon — location-based scheduling; exact page citation not confirmed in available PMI search results

Core Meaning: A dependency between two activities or between an activity and a milestone.
Expansion: Logical relationships define how one schedule element relates to another in time and sequence. The knowledgebase identifies them as dependencies between activities or between an activity and a milestone. They are fundamental to building a realistic schedule network and understanding how changes affect downstream work.
Contrast: Dependency — logical relationship is the specific scheduling link; dependency is the broader concept of one element relying on another.
Example: Testing cannot begin until coding is complete, so the schedule links the two activities with a logical relationship.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase / Logical relationship

Core Meaning: A near-term planning practice that refines upcoming work so it is ready to be executed reliably.
Expansion: Lookahead planning focuses on the next short planning horizon, often a few weeks, to confirm that upcoming tasks are feasible, sequenced, and free of known constraints. It bridges high-level schedule intent with executable field or team-level commitments. PMI practice uses the concept especially in construction and flow-based delivery, but the retrieved sources do not show it as a formal PMI glossary entry.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Lookahead planning is a rolling, short-horizon planning process that turns broader schedule logic into ready work. It checks upcoming activities for prerequisites such as approvals, materials, decisions, labor availability, and access so teams do not commit to work that is not actually executable.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because many schedule failures happen not from bad master plans but from weak preparation of near-term work. It is especially useful in construction, implementation, maintenance shutdowns, and other environments where coordination, constraints, and handoffs determine daily reliability.

The most common misconception: that lookahead planning is just a more detailed weekly to-do list. In practice, it is a constraint-removal and readiness discipline that improves workflow quality before work starts, not merely a restatement of activities already on the schedule.

How it shows up in real projects: A site team reviews the next three weeks of work, identifies missing permits and late material deliveries, and resequences tasks before crews are stranded. A technology rollout team uses a rolling lookahead to confirm environment readiness, business signoffs, and test data availability before each deployment window.
Contrast: Master schedule — lookahead planning prepares the next executable window of work; a master schedule shows the broader end-to-end timing of the project.
Example: A hospital renovation team uses a three-week lookahead to verify room access, infection-control approvals, and equipment delivery before committing crews to the next area.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A relationship that is contractually required or inherent in the nature of the work.
Expansion: Mandatory dependencies are sequencing relationships that cannot be changed without violating physical reality, legal constraints, or contractual obligations. They are often described as hard logic because they are not based on team preference. These dependencies matter in schedule development because they restrict flexibility in activity sequencing.
Contrast: Discretionary dependency — a mandatory dependency is required by the work or contract; a discretionary dependency is chosen by the team as a preferred sequence.
Example: On a bridge project, concrete curing must finish before load testing can begin.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, mandatory dependency, page 716

Core Meaning: A summary-level project schedule that identifies major deliverables, work breakdown structure components, and key schedule milestones.
Expansion: A master schedule provides a high-level view of the project timeline rather than a detailed activity-by-activity plan. It is used to communicate major timing points, key dependencies, and deliverable flow to sponsors, governance bodies, and other stakeholders. In larger efforts, it can integrate multiple subprojects into one schedule view.
Contrast: Milestone schedule — a master schedule summarizes major deliverables, components, and milestones across the project; a milestone schedule focuses specifically on milestones and planned dates.
Example: A transformation office uses a master schedule to show design completion, vendor onboarding, data migration, testing, and go-live across several interrelated workstreams.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, glossary term master schedule, page 716

Core Meaning: A point in a schedule network where multiple parallel paths come together into a single path.
Expansion: A merge point occurs when two or more predecessor paths converge on one activity. It matters because delay on any incoming path can affect the merged activity and downstream schedule performance.
Contrast: Path divergence — a merge point brings parallel paths together; a path divergence splits one path into multiple paths.
Example: In a software release schedule, testing, security review, and user documentation all merge before final deployment approval.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — merge point, exact page not available in provided results

Core Meaning: A significant point or event in a project, program, or portfolio.
Expansion: A milestone marks an important achievement, decision point, or transition such as approval, completion of a major deliverable, or phase end. It has no duration in the schedule even though it may trigger substantial downstream work. PMI uses milestones to support planning, tracking, reporting, and governance across predictive, hybrid, and adaptive environments.
Deep Dive:
A milestone is a zero-duration marker used to represent a notable event or condition in the lifecycle of work. It helps teams signal progress, readiness, or the completion of a key commitment.

Why it matters and where it applies: milestones matter because they create shared visibility for stakeholders and provide anchor points for governance, dependency management, and benefits tracking. They are used in schedules, roadmaps, release plans, contracts, and executive reports across nearly every delivery approach.

The most common misconception is that a milestone is just any task with high importance. A milestone is not work itself; it is the marker that a meaningful point has been reached, often after one or more tasks or deliverables are completed.

In real projects, teams use milestones for events such as:

- Charter approval.
- Design sign-off.
- Test environment readiness.
- Regulatory submission.
- Go-live.
- Handover.

These points often become review gates or communication checkpoints for sponsors and customers.
Contrast: Activity — an activity is scheduled work with duration; a milestone is a zero-duration marker for a significant event or achievement.
Example: On an ERP implementation, the team sets data migration sign-off as a milestone that must be reached before user acceptance testing can begin.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, glossary term milestone, page 244

Core Meaning: A project document that identifies all project milestones and normally indicates whether each is mandatory or optional.
Expansion: The milestone list records the key events that stakeholders will monitor throughout the project. It is often aligned to governance gates, major deliverables, contractual dates, or external commitments.
Contrast: Schedule milestone — a schedule milestone is the individual event marker; the milestone list is the document that compiles those markers.
Example: A public-sector project keeps a milestone list showing procurement award, design approval, legislative review, pilot launch, and final rollout, with each tagged as mandatory or optional.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, glossary term milestone list, page 720

Core Meaning: A milestone schedule is a summary-level schedule that identifies major milestones and planned dates for significant events or deliverables in a project.
Expansion: A milestone schedule presents key points in time such as phase completions, approvals, external commitments, and major deliverable dates without showing the full detail of all activities. It is used to communicate high-level timing expectations to sponsors, customers, and governance bodies. In PMI practice, it supports alignment and oversight by highlighting the dates that matter most for control, reporting, and decision making.
Contrast: Project schedule — a milestone schedule shows only major checkpoints and target dates; a project schedule includes the broader set of activities, durations, dependencies, and timing details.
Example: In a construction project, the milestone schedule shows permit approval, site mobilisation, foundation completion, structural completion, inspection sign-off, and handover dates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, milestone schedule, page 715

Core Meaning: The realistic estimate of an activity's duration assuming normal conditions and resources.
Expansion: Most likely duration (tM) is one of the three estimates used in three-point estimating and PERT, alongside the optimistic and pessimistic durations. It represents the duration expected under typical, realistic conditions, accounting for the resources likely to be assigned and the usual interruptions. Combined with the other two estimates, it feeds the triangular or beta (PERT) distribution to produce a more reliable expected duration.
Contrast: Optimistic duration - the most likely value assumes normal conditions, whereas the optimistic value assumes the best-case scenario.
Example: Estimating a test cycle at 10 days as the most likely duration, with 7 days optimistic and 16 days pessimistic.
Source: PMBOK Guide - three-point estimating

Core Meaning: An activity with total float that is considered low based on expert judgment.
Expansion: A near-critical activity is not on the critical path, but it has little scheduling flexibility. Because its float is low, a small delay could make it critical or affect the project finish date. The threshold for low float is set through expert judgment in the project context.
Contrast: Critical activity — a near-critical activity still has some float remaining; a critical activity has no schedule flexibility that protects the project finish date.
Example: In a system rollout, user acceptance testing has only one day of float, so the project manager tracks it as near-critical because a minor delay could affect deployment.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — near-critical activity

Core Meaning: A sequence of activities with low float that can become a critical path if the float is exhausted.
Expansion: A near-critical path is a chain of scheduled activities that sits close to the critical path because it has limited float. Delays along this path can quickly consume that float and change which path controls project completion. It is therefore important in schedule monitoring and forecasting.
Contrast: Critical path — a near-critical path is close to becoming schedule-driving; the critical path already determines the shortest possible project duration.
Example: In a construction project, procurement, delivery, and installation of custom glass are tracked as a near-critical path because even a short shipping delay could make that sequence schedule-driving.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — near-critical path

Core Meaning: The amount by which an activity's required dates exceed its available dates, signaling the schedule is behind a constraint.
Expansion: Negative float (or negative slack) occurs when a schedule constraint, such as an imposed finish date, cannot be met given the current network logic, so an activity's early dates fall later than its late dates. It indicates the schedule is infeasible as planned and that time must be recovered through compression, re-sequencing, or renegotiating the constraint. It is revealed during critical path analysis.
Contrast: Total float - positive total float is spare time available, whereas negative float is a time deficit that must be eliminated.
Example: A milestone imposed two weeks earlier than the network supports produces minus 10 days of float on the path leading to it.
Source: PMBOK Guide / Practice Standard for Scheduling - float

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of project activities and the logical relationships among them.
Expansion: A network diagram shows project activities in sequence and connects them through dependencies so the team can understand workflow logic. PMI uses it as a core scheduling representation for analysing activity order, identifying the critical path, and supporting schedule development. It is foundational because many schedule-management techniques depend on understanding these logical relationships clearly.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A network diagram is a visual model of the activities needed to complete project work and the dependencies that link them. It shows which tasks must precede or follow others and how work flows from project start to finish.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is central to schedule planning because it supports sequence definition, critical path analysis, float analysis, and impact assessment when dates shift. It applies across predictive scheduling and is especially valuable in projects with many interdependent activities such as construction, engineering, infrastructure, and system implementation.

The most common misconception: that a network diagram is simply another timeline view. In fact, its main purpose is not calendar display but logical dependency analysis, which is why it differs from a Gantt chart.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use it to map predecessors and successors, identify critical activities, test schedule scenarios, and understand the downstream effect of delays before committing to a baseline.
Contrast: Gantt chart — a network diagram emphasises activity logic and dependency relationships, while a Gantt chart emphasises timing and calendar placement.
Example: A construction project team maps excavation, foundation work, framing, inspections, and utilities in a network diagram to determine the critical path and evaluate the effect of permit delays.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Network diagram, page 14

Core Meaning: All activity dependencies in a project schedule network diagram.
Expansion: Network logic is the full pattern of dependency relationships that connects project activities. It expresses the required sequencing among tasks, including predecessor and successor links. This logic supports schedule analysis, path calculation, and identification of schedule sensitivity.
Contrast: Schedule network diagram — network logic is the dependency structure itself; the schedule network diagram is the visual model that displays that structure.
Example: In a facility upgrade, the rule that electrical inspection must occur before ceiling closure is part of the project network logic.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — network logic

Core Meaning: An invalid schedule path that returns through the same node, which standard network analysis cannot resolve.
Expansion: A network loop is a sequence of activities and dependencies that circles back to revisit a node already on the path. Because the critical path method assumes a one-directional flow toward project end, loops create logical contradictions and cannot be analyzed with traditional schedule techniques, so they must be removed or modeled differently.
Contrast: Network path - a valid network path flows in one direction to project end, whereas a loop doubles back on itself.
Example: Activity B depends on A, C depends on B, and A is set to depend on C, forming an unresolvable loop.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule network analysis

Core Meaning: A sequence of schedule activities linked by logical relationships in a project schedule network diagram.
Expansion: A network path shows one route through the scheduled work from one point in the project to another. Project managers use paths in network analysis to understand sequencing, identify schedule sensitivity, and determine which path is critical or near critical. In PMI usage, it is a scheduling concept tied to dependency logic rather than a general description of workflow.
Contrast: Critical path — A network path is any logically connected path through the schedule network; the critical path is the path with the least total float that determines the project's shortest completion time.
Example: In a construction schedule, the path from foundation work to framing to roofing to final inspection forms one network path used in schedule analysis.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Glossary, term Network Path

Core Meaning: A point in a schedule network diagram at which one or more dependencies intersect.
Expansion: A node represents an activity or event depending on the diagramming method used. It helps show how work is connected within the project schedule model.
Contrast: Activity — a node is the diagram element used to represent work or an event; an activity is the actual scheduled work item.
Example: In a precedence diagramming method network, each task box shown for design, build, and test is a node linked by dependencies.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Project schedule network diagram terms, p. 243

Core Meaning: The estimated time associated with a node in a network model, typically representing the duration of the activity shown there.
Expansion: Node duration is used in network-based schedule representations to indicate how long the represented work is expected to take. It supports sequencing and path analysis across the schedule.
Contrast: Elapsed time — node duration is the planned duration of the represented activity; elapsed time is the actual calendar passage from start to finish.
Example: A scheduler labels the coding node with a duration of five working days to calculate downstream dates.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A dependency that is required by the nature of the work or by contract.
Expansion: A non-discretionary dependency is a mandatory sequencing relationship that cannot be freely rearranged without violating technical, physical, legal, or contractual constraints. PMI commonly describes it as mandatory logic. It matters because these dependencies limit schedule flexibility and often shape the critical path.
Contrast: Discretionary dependency — a non-discretionary dependency is mandatory because of the work or constraints; a discretionary dependency is chosen by the team as a preferred sequence.
Example: Concrete must cure before structural loading can begin, so the successor work has a non-discretionary dependency on the pour activity.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, non-discretionary dependency, p. 715

Core Meaning: The best-case estimate of an activity's duration assuming everything goes well.
Expansion: Optimistic duration (tO) is the shortest time an activity could take if all favorable conditions hold and no problems arise. It is one of the three inputs to three-point estimating and PERT, used with the most likely and pessimistic values to model the uncertainty in a duration estimate.
Contrast: Pessimistic duration - the optimistic value assumes the best case, whereas the pessimistic value assumes the worst case.
Example: Estimating that coding could finish in 7 days if no blockers appear.
Source: PMBOK Guide - three-point estimating

Core Meaning: Unjustified extra time or cost secretly added to an estimate to feel safe.
Expansion: Padding is the practice of inflating an estimate with hidden contingency beyond what analysis justifies, simply to give the estimator a comfort margin. Unlike transparent reserves, padding is concealed and undermines trust, schedule realism, and the ability to manage true uncertainty. It also tends to trigger behaviors like student syndrome that consume the slack anyway.
Contrast: Contingency reserve - a contingency reserve is a transparent, analyzed allowance for identified risk, whereas padding is hidden, arbitrary buffer.
Example: An engineer quietly doubling a 5-day estimate to 10 days just in case, without explaining why.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Work expands to fill the time allotted to it.
Expansion: Parkinson's law states that a task will swell in perceived effort and duration to consume whatever time is made available for it. In project estimating it warns that overly generous durations get used up regardless of the real work needed, eroding schedule efficiency and feeding behaviors like padding and student syndrome.
Contrast: Student syndrome - Parkinson's law says work stretches to fill the time, whereas student syndrome is delaying effort until just before the deadline.
Example: A task that genuinely needs three days takes the full two weeks scheduled for it.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A relationship in which a schedule activity has more than one predecessor.
Expansion: Path convergence occurs when multiple activity paths join before a successor activity can start. It increases schedule sensitivity because delays in any predecessor can affect the converging activity.
Contrast: Path divergence — path convergence brings multiple predecessors into one activity; path divergence sends one activity to multiple successors.
Example: System integration testing can begin only after both application coding and test environment setup are complete.
Source: PMBOK Guide — path convergence, p. 127

Core Meaning: A relationship in which a schedule activity has more than one successor.
Expansion: Path divergence occurs when one completed activity branches into several downstream activities. It matters because slippage in that activity can affect multiple successor paths at once.
Contrast: Path convergence — path divergence branches one activity to multiple successors; path convergence joins multiple predecessors into one activity.
Example: Once design approval is finished, the project launches procurement, development, and training-preparation activities in parallel.
Source: PMBOK Guide — path divergence, p. 127

Core Meaning: The ratio of work done to total work on an activity or component, expressed as a percentage.
Expansion: Percent complete is a progress measure comparing the estimated work accomplished to the total estimated work for an activity or WBS component. It is used to report status and, in earned value management, to help determine earned value. Because it can rely on subjective judgment, organizations often use rules like fixed-formula or weighted milestones to make it more objective.
Contrast: Earned value - percent complete is a progress percentage, whereas earned value translates that progress into a budgeted dollar amount.
Example: Reporting an activity as 60 percent complete in a weekly status update.
Source: PMBOK Guide - earned value / progress measurement

Core Meaning: The worst-case estimate of an activity's duration assuming problems arise.
Expansion: Pessimistic duration (tP) is the longest time an activity could reasonably take if unfavorable conditions and known risks materialize. It is one of the three estimates in three-point estimating and PERT, used with the optimistic and most likely values to capture the range of uncertainty in a duration.
Contrast: Optimistic duration - the pessimistic value assumes the worst case, whereas the optimistic value assumes the best case.
Example: Estimating that integration could take 16 days if major defects surface.
Source: PMBOK Guide - three-point estimating

Core Meaning: A schedule network diagramming technique in which activities are represented by nodes and linked by logical relationships.
Expansion: Precedence Diagramming Method is a scheduling technique used to model the sequence of project activities with nodes and dependency arrows. It supports relationship types such as finish-to-start, start-to-start, finish-to-finish, and start-to-finish. PMI uses it to develop schedule logic, analyse dependencies, and support critical path analysis.
Contrast: Activity-on-arrow — Precedence Diagramming Method places activities in nodes and uses arrows for relationships; activity-on-arrow places activities on arrows.
Example: A software implementation schedule uses Precedence Diagramming Method to show that training can start once core configuration begins, while user acceptance testing cannot finish until defect fixes are complete.
