Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Precedence Diagramming Method, page 699

Core Meaning: A precedence relationship is the logical dependency that determines the sequence in which activities occur in a schedule network.
Expansion: Precedence relationships connect predecessor and successor activities so the project team can model how work flows through the schedule. PMI uses them in schedule network analysis through relationship types such as finish-to-start, finish-to-finish, start-to-start, and start-to-finish. They are essential for identifying critical paths, evaluating schedule changes, and understanding how delays propagate.
Contrast: Dependency — a precedence relationship is the specific logical link used in the schedule network between activities, while dependency is the broader concept of one element relying on another.
Example: In a facility-upgrade project, server-rack installation cannot start until room-cooling modifications are completed, creating a finish-to-start precedence relationship.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) — Sixth Edition, section 6.3 Sequence Activities and glossary entries for precedence diagramming relationships, p. 189

Core Meaning: An activity that logically comes before another activity in the schedule and influences when that later activity can start or finish.
Expansion: A predecessor is the earlier activity in a logical relationship between scheduled activities. It provides the basis for sequencing work and determining dependencies in the schedule model. PMI uses predecessor relationships to analyse timing, identify the critical path, and understand how delays can propagate through the project.
Contrast: Successor — a predecessor comes before and constrains a later activity; a successor is the activity that follows.
Example: In an office-fitout project, 'Complete electrical rough-in' is the predecessor to 'Install drywall' because the walls cannot be closed before the wiring work is finished.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Predecessor activity, page 214

Core Meaning: An activity that logically comes before a dependent activity in a schedule.
Expansion: A predecessor activity is the earlier activity in a logical relationship. It determines when a successor activity can start or finish, depending on the dependency type. Predecessors are fundamental to schedule network logic and critical path analysis.
Contrast: Successor activity — a predecessor comes before; a successor comes after.
Example: Foundation excavation is the predecessor to concrete pouring in a construction schedule.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Predecessor activity, p. 127

Core Meaning: Production rate is the quantity of work, units, or deliverable output produced in a given period of time.
Expansion: In project management, production rate is a planning and control measure used to estimate duration, resource needs, and work performance. It is common in construction, manufacturing, installation, and other execution-heavy environments where repeatable output can be measured per hour, shift, or day. Project teams use it to forecast progress, compare planned versus actual performance, and adjust capacity or methods when throughput changes.
Contrast: Productivity — production rate measures output over time, while productivity usually compares output to the resources or effort used to produce it.
Example: A construction project estimates that a crew can install 120 square meters of flooring per day and uses that production rate to schedule the finishing work.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A calendar that identifies working days and shifts available for scheduled activities.
Expansion: A project calendar defines when project work can be performed. It captures working time, nonworking time, and shifts that affect schedule calculations. It is used with activity durations and resource availability to build realistic schedules.
Contrast: Resource calendar — a project calendar applies to the project schedule overall; a resource calendar applies to a specific resource or resource group.
Example: A construction project calendar excludes weekends and public holidays, so activity dates are calculated only on working days.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Project calendars, p. 81

Core Meaning: The amount of time a whole project can slip without missing an externally imposed or customer-committed delivery date.
Expansion: Project float (sometimes called project slack) is the difference between the project's calculated completion date from the schedule network and an externally imposed deadline that is later than that date. It differs from activity float because it applies to the entire project rather than a single activity. Positive project float gives the team a buffer against delays; if the imposed date is earlier than the calculated finish, project float is negative and the schedule must be compressed.
Contrast: Free float - free float is the delay an activity can absorb without affecting its successor, whereas project float concerns the entire project against an imposed end date.
Example: A schedule network shows completion on June 1 but the customer's contracted date is June 15, giving 14 days of project float.
Source: Practice Standard for Scheduling / PMBOK Guide - float concepts

Core Meaning: An output of a schedule model that presents linked activities with planned dates, durations, milestones, and resources.
Expansion: The project schedule shows when project activities are planned to occur and how they are sequenced. It is produced from the schedule model and reflects planned dates, durations, milestones, and resource assignments. PMI treats it as a key planning and control artifact for coordinating work over time. It supports execution, monitoring, forecasting, and communication across stakeholders.
Contrast: Schedule model — the schedule model is the analytical representation used to calculate timing; the project schedule is the resulting time-based plan used for management and communication.
Example: For a bridge repair project, the project schedule lays out inspection, design, permitting, material delivery, lane closures, repair work, and reopening milestones.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition and PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Project schedule, page 271

Core Meaning: Project Schedule Management is the knowledge area that includes the processes required to plan, develop, manage, execute, and control the project schedule.
Expansion: Project Schedule Management covers how the project team defines activities, sequences work, estimates durations, develops the schedule, and controls schedule performance over time. In PMI terms, it is not just the timeline itself but the full set of management processes and practices used to produce and maintain a realistic schedule. It is foundational because time performance affects delivery commitments, resource coordination, stakeholder expectations, and often cost and risk outcomes. It applies across predictive, hybrid, and adaptive environments, though the techniques and level of detail differ by approach.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Project Schedule Management is the body of project management work focused on turning scope into a time-based delivery plan and then keeping that plan useful as the project proceeds. It includes defining activities, sequencing them, estimating durations, building the schedule model, and controlling schedule changes and performance.

Why it matters and where it applies: schedules coordinate dependencies, resource timing, milestones, and stakeholder commitments. Without schedule management, teams may still work hard, but they are far less likely to align on when value will be delivered, where delays are emerging, and what corrective action is needed.

The most common misconception: that schedule management is just making a Gantt chart at the start of the project. In practice, PMI treats it as an ongoing management discipline that combines planning, analysis, monitoring, forecasting, and decision-making throughout the project life cycle.

How it shows up in real projects — teams produce artifacts such as:
- Milestone lists
- Activity sequences
- Duration estimates
- Critical path analysis
- Sprint or release timing
- Baseline comparisons
- Recovery plans when slippage occurs

Sponsors and stakeholders often experience it through status dates, forecast completion changes, and milestone commitments.
Contrast: Project schedule — Project Schedule Management is the management discipline and set of processes; the project schedule is the resulting time-based model and output.
Example: On a facility-upgrade project, the team sequences permitting, procurement, installation, and testing work, identifies the critical path, tracks milestone slippage weekly, and replans around a delayed equipment delivery.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 6, pp. 173-174

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of the logical relationships among the schedule activities of a project.
Expansion: A Project Schedule Network Diagram shows activities as nodes or arrows and displays the precedence relationships that determine sequencing. It is used to analyse dependencies, identify possible parallel work, and support methods such as critical path analysis. The diagram helps the team understand how schedule logic drives start and finish dates rather than treating the schedule as a simple list of tasks.
Contrast: Gantt chart — a network diagram emphasises logical dependencies among activities; a Gantt chart primarily emphasises timing and duration on a calendar scale.
Example: For a data-centre migration, the team maps server build, security review, data replication, cutover rehearsal, and production switch activities in a network diagram to identify the critical path and where parallel work is possible.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), page citation needed from PMI source

Core Meaning: The point in time when project work is planned or authorized to begin.
Expansion: The project start date marks the beginning of the project timeline, against which durations and milestones are measured. It is set during schedule development and may be driven by an imposed constraint such as a contract or funding date.
Contrast: Distinct from the project finish date, which marks planned completion, and together they bound the overall duration.
Example: A contract requires work to commence on the first of March, fixing the project start date as a constraint.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A planning approach where work is scheduled backward from a target milestone based on what downstream activities actually need.
Expansion: Pull scheduling, common in Lean and the Last Planner System, builds a plan by starting from a completion milestone and having teams define handoffs based on what the next activity requires, pulling work rather than pushing it from a central master schedule. It increases reliability and ownership because those doing the work commit to their own handoffs.
Contrast: Critical path method - CPM pushes a centrally computed forward-pass schedule, while pull scheduling derives commitments backward from the milestone based on downstream need.
Example: In a construction pull-planning session, trades work backward from the handover date, each committing to the predecessors the next trade needs.
Source: Lean Construction - Last Planner System

Core Meaning: Estimating techniques that rely on expert judgement, relative comparison, or descriptive assessment rather than detailed quantitative calculation.
Expansion: These techniques are useful when information is limited, speed matters, or early planning does not support precise numeric analysis. They often draw on analogy, experience, pattern recognition, and comparative sizing to produce estimates that are directionally useful. PMI treats them as practical tools for forming reasonable expectations when the project team lacks enough detail for more rigorous estimating.
Contrast: Quantitative estimating techniques — qualitative estimating techniques rely more on judgement and relative assessment, while quantitative techniques depend more on numerical models, detailed data, or statistical calculation.
Example: Early in a digital-transformation project, the team judges that a customer-portal feature is roughly twice the effort of a previously delivered feature and uses that comparison to size the work.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge — Qualitative estimating techniques, p. 170

Core Meaning: The estimated time still needed to finish an in-progress activity.
Expansion: Remaining duration is the time required to complete the unfinished portion of an activity from the status date forward. It is used to update the schedule model and forecast completion as work progresses.
Contrast: Distinct from actual duration, which records time already spent, remaining duration looks forward to what is left.
Example: An activity planned for ten days is half done at day six, so its remaining duration is revised to four days.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: An iterative planning technique in which near-term work is planned in detail while future work is planned at a higher level.
Expansion: Rolling wave planning is a form of progressive elaboration that plans imminent work in detail and leaves later work at a broader level until more is known. It is useful when future tasks are uncertain, likely to change, or depend on learning from earlier work. PMI treats it as a disciplined way to balance planning precision with adaptability across the life cycle.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Rolling wave planning is a planning approach that deliberately varies the level of detail over time. Teams define upcoming work packages or activities precisely, while keeping later work summarized until it moves closer to execution.

Why it matters and where it applies: it prevents false precision and reduces rework caused by planning too far ahead with weak information. It is especially useful in long, complex, innovative, or change-prone projects where later scope, sequencing, or resource needs will become clearer only after earlier work is completed.

The most common misconception: that it means weak planning or a lack of discipline. In reality, it is structured planning with an intentional decision to delay detail until the information quality justifies it.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may fully schedule the next release, phase, or sprint set while representing later work only as milestones or high-level deliverables. As the project progresses, those later items are decomposed into detailed activities, estimates, and assignments.
Contrast: Progressive elaboration — rolling wave planning is the specific technique of detailing near-term work first; progressive elaboration is the broader concept of refining plans as information improves.
Example: A product development team plans the next two sprints at task level but keeps the following quarter at feature level until user testing and technical discovery reduce uncertainty.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition — Rolling wave planning

Core Meaning: The planned dates for performing project activities and achieving milestones.
Expansion: Schedule is the time-based plan for when project work will occur. In PMI usage, it ties activities, durations, dependencies, and milestones into a coordinated timeline for execution and control. It is the basis for monitoring progress and comparing actual performance against planned timing.
Contrast: Schedule model — Schedule is the resulting time plan; the schedule model is the representation used to develop and analyse it.
Example: A software release project publishes a timeline showing design, build, test, and deployment dates across a twelve-week delivery window.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Schedule, page not provided in the search results

Core Meaning: A distinct piece of work in the schedule that is planned, sequenced, and tracked to produce project output.
Expansion: A schedule activity is the basic unit of work used in schedule planning and control. It is defined with enough detail to estimate duration, assign relationships, and monitor progress. PMI uses schedule activities to build the schedule model and support analysis such as sequencing, estimating, and tracking progress.
Contrast: Work package — a schedule activity is a schedulable unit of work, while a work package is a scope component that may contain one or more activities.
Example: In a data-centre relocation project, 'Install server racks' is defined as a schedule activity with predecessors, a planned duration, and assigned technicians.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Schedule activity, page 197

Core Meaning: A condition accepted as true for the purpose of developing, estimating, or analysing the project schedule.
Expansion: Schedule assumptions are planning conditions the team accepts when building or maintaining the schedule, such as resource availability, productivity rates, approval turnaround times, or vendor lead times. They matter because planned dates and sequencing often depend on them being true. In PMI practice, assumptions should be documented and monitored because when they prove false, schedule risk and reforecasting usually follow.
Contrast: Constraint — an assumption is treated as true for planning purposes; a constraint is a limiting factor that restricts schedule options.
Example: A software project schedule assumes the security reviewer is available every Friday, but when that reviewer becomes unavailable the testing and release dates slip.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The approved version of the schedule model that is used as the basis for comparison to actual results.
Expansion: The schedule baseline is the formally accepted project schedule against which progress and performance are measured. It is not updated casually; approved changes are made through change control. It helps the team determine whether work is ahead of, on, or behind plan and supports forecasting and corrective action.
Contrast: Schedule model — the schedule model is the working representation of planned activities and dates; the schedule baseline is the approved reference version used for control.
Example: After the steering committee approves the implementation timeline, the project manager uses that approved plan as the schedule baseline to track slippage in testing and deployment.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process Groups A Practice Guide — Schedule baseline, page 170

Core Meaning: A technique used to shorten the schedule duration without reducing project scope.
Expansion: Schedule compression is applied when the project must finish earlier than planned without cutting agreed scope. It shortens overall duration by changing sequencing, resource use, or both. PMI recognises it as a schedule development and control technique, commonly through methods such as crashing and fast tracking.
Contrast: Crashing — schedule compression is the broader category; crashing is one specific way to compress a schedule by adding resources or cost.
Example: A data-centre migration project overlaps environment validation with late-stage build work and adds specialist staff to critical activities so the go-live date can be pulled forward.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, schedule compression, page 196

Core Meaning: A schedule restriction that limits when activities, milestones, or the overall project can start or finish.
Expansion: A schedule constraint sets imposed dates or timing restrictions on planned work. In PMI usage, these constraints can affect sequence logic, float, and schedule flexibility because they restrict how the schedule model can calculate dates. Typical examples include mandatory completion dates, externally imposed milestones, and start-no-earlier-than conditions.
Contrast: Schedule assumption — a schedule constraint is a limiting condition that must be respected, while a schedule assumption is a planning factor believed to be true for scheduling purposes.
Example: A regulatory submission project must finish system validation before a fixed government filing window closes, so the filing date acts as a schedule constraint.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, page 191

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring the status of the project to update the project schedule and manage changes to the schedule baseline.
Expansion: Schedule control is the ongoing work of comparing actual progress with the approved schedule, determining the causes and extent of variance, and taking action to keep or return the project to an acceptable trajectory. PMI places it after schedule development and links it to integrated change control, forecasting, and performance analysis rather than simple date tracking. It helps teams detect slippage early, evaluate options, and manage approved changes in a disciplined way.
Contrast: Schedule development — schedule development creates the approved schedule model and baseline; schedule control monitors performance against that baseline and manages change.
Example: A product-launch project reviews milestone slippage each week, analyses dependency impacts, approves a revised sequence for testing and training, and updates the schedule baseline through change control.
Source: PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Control Schedule, pp. 222-230

Core Meaning: The collection of information used to describe and control the project schedule.
Expansion: Schedule data is the supporting information behind the schedule model, including milestones, activities, attributes, assumptions, and constraints, along with items such as resource histograms and alternative schedules. It provides the detail needed to analyze, report, and control the schedule over the life of the project.
Contrast: Project schedule - the project schedule is the output that presents planned dates, whereas schedule data is the broader supporting information behind it.
Example: The resource requirements, scheduling assumptions, and contingency reserves stored alongside a project's network diagram.
Source: PMBOK Guide - schedule data

Core Meaning: The process of analyzing activity sequences, durations, resource requirements, and schedule constraints to create the project schedule model.
Expansion: Schedule development converts planned activities and sequencing logic into a workable schedule model. It integrates duration estimates, calendars, resource availability, dependencies, and constraints to determine planned dates for project work. In PMI practice, it is a core scheduling activity used to produce and refine the schedule baseline and forecast completion dates.
Contrast: Scheduling — Schedule development is the specific process of producing the schedule model; scheduling is the broader act or discipline of arranging work over time.
Example: A construction project team sequences foundation, framing, and inspection activities, applies crew calendars and dependency logic, and develops a schedule showing forecast start and finish dates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, Section 6.5 Schedule Development

Core Meaning: The predicted amount of time needed to complete an activity, work package, or project under stated assumptions.
Expansion: A schedule estimate expresses expected duration and may be produced using expert judgment, analogous data, parametric methods, or three-point estimating. In PMI usage, it supports the development of activity durations and the overall schedule model rather than serving as the schedule itself. Its usefulness depends on the quality of scope definition, resource assumptions, and estimating basis.
Contrast: Duration estimate — a schedule estimate focuses on expected time for planned work in the schedule context; a schedule is the time-phased model of when work will occur.
Example: For a data-migration activity, the team estimates ten working days based on prior migrations, available specialists, and the expected number of records to convert.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Predictions of future schedule outcomes based on current progress, performance trends, and known conditions.
Expansion: Schedule forecasts estimate likely completion dates or milestone outcomes using actual performance and current project information. They support monitoring and controlling work by showing where the schedule is trending rather than only where it was originally planned to go. Teams use them to anticipate slippage, evaluate corrective action, and communicate likely timing impacts before missed dates become final facts.
Contrast: Schedule baseline — the schedule baseline is the approved time plan; schedule forecasts are forward-looking estimates of where performance is likely to finish.
Example: After repeated late vendor responses, the project manager forecasts that user acceptance testing will start two weeks later than planned unless procurement issues are resolved.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The processes, methods, and controls used to plan, develop, monitor, and manage the project schedule.
Expansion: Schedule management covers how project activities are sequenced, estimated, modelled, baselined, and controlled so work can be completed on time. In PMI usage, it includes both creating the schedule and managing schedule performance as the project progresses. It is foundational because time is one of the core project constraints and schedule decisions directly affect cost, risk, resources, and stakeholder expectations.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Schedule management is the disciplined management of project time through planning, schedule development, and ongoing control. It turns scope into a time-based roadmap by defining activities, sequencing dependencies, estimating durations, and setting dates.

Why it matters and where it applies: nearly every project depends on coordinated timing across tasks, people, vendors, approvals, and milestones. Strong schedule management improves forecast accuracy, exposes critical path pressure, and helps teams respond early when slippage threatens delivery commitments.

The most common misconception: that schedule management is just maintaining a Gantt chart. In practice, it is a broader management function that includes governance, estimation assumptions, dependency logic, progress measurement, change control, and schedule performance analysis.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team establishes scheduling rules in the schedule management plan, builds a network-based schedule model, baselines key milestones, tracks actual progress, analyses variance, and adjusts forecasts when emerging delays appear.
Contrast: Schedule management plan — schedule management is the overall function of planning and controlling time performance; the schedule management plan is the document that defines how that function will be performed.
Example: On a data-center migration project, the team defines installation, testing, and cutover activities, sequences handoffs between vendors, baselines the timeline, and tracks weekly variance against milestone dates.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition / PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition — Schedule Management Plan, Project Schedule Management knowledge area, not enough verified page detail available from retrieved PMI content

Core Meaning: A component of the project management plan that defines how the project schedule will be developed, monitored, and controlled.
Expansion: The schedule management plan sets the rules for managing time on the project rather than listing the dates themselves. It typically defines the scheduling method, level of detail, units of measure, control thresholds, update frequency, and reporting expectations. PMI treats it as foundational because it establishes the governance that makes scheduling consistent, measurable, and controllable across the life of the project.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The schedule management plan is a subsidiary plan that explains how schedule work will be performed and governed. It provides the framework for developing the schedule model, maintaining the baseline, and handling updates and changes.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects need more than a list of activities and dates; they need agreed rules for how time will be estimated, tracked, reported, and controlled. This matters on both predictive and hybrid projects, especially where many teams or suppliers contribute schedule data.

The most common misconception: that it is the same thing as the project schedule. It is not the timeline itself; it is the management approach that defines how the timeline is built and kept under control.

How it shows up in real projects: the plan may specify the scheduling tool, calendar conventions, progress-measurement rules, variance thresholds, cadence for updates, and the approval path for schedule changes before the working schedule is baselined and maintained.
Contrast: Project schedule — the schedule management plan defines how schedule management will work; the project schedule is the actual dated model of activities and milestones.
Example: On a construction programme, the schedule management plan defines weekly progress updates, milestone tolerance limits, and the approval workflow for any change to the critical path.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process group practice for plan schedule management — exact page citation unavailable in retrieved context

Core Meaning: A representation of the project plan that shows how and when activities will be performed.
Expansion: The schedule model shows activities, durations, dependencies, milestones, and planned dates in a structured time-based representation. It is used to analyse sequencing, forecast completion, and evaluate the impact of changes. Once approved for control purposes, a version of it becomes the schedule baseline.
Contrast: Schedule baseline — the schedule model is the planning and analysis representation; the schedule baseline is the approved version used to measure performance.
Example: A construction team uses a schedule model with linked design, permitting, procurement, and build activities to test whether the target opening date is feasible.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process Groups A Practice Guide — Schedule model, page 171

Core Meaning: Examining the schedule model to evaluate its logic, durations, and feasibility.
Expansion: Schedule model analysis investigates the network of activities, dependencies, durations, and constraints to confirm the schedule is realistic and to surface risks such as negative float or over-allocated resources. Techniques include critical path analysis, what-if scenario analysis, and resource optimization. The analysis supports forecasting completion dates and testing the impact of proposed changes.
Contrast: Unlike simple progress tracking, which records what has happened, schedule model analysis interrogates the underlying model to predict outcomes and test alternatives.
Example: A planner runs a what-if scenario in the schedule model to see whether fast-tracking two activities recovers a slipped milestone.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A technique used to generate the project schedule model by analysing activity sequences, durations, resource requirements, and schedule constraints.
Expansion: Schedule network analysis examines the logical relationships among activities to determine feasible timing and overall schedule behavior. PMI associates it with methods such as critical path analysis, critical chain method, what-if scenario analysis, and resource optimization techniques used to assess float, sequencing, and completion dates. It supports schedule development and control by revealing which activities drive timing risk and where flexibility exists.
Contrast: Critical path method — critical path method is one technique used within schedule network analysis; schedule network analysis is the broader analytical process.
Example: A data-center migration team models dependencies among procurement, rack installation, network configuration, testing, and cutover activities to identify the critical path and assess the impact of a delayed hardware delivery.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Schedule network analysis; PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Section 6.5, p. 196

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of the logical relationships among project schedule activities.
Expansion: A schedule network diagram shows activities and the dependencies that connect them. It helps the team analyse sequence, identify critical and near-critical paths, and understand how delays can propagate through the schedule. PMI treats it as a core representation for schedule logic and timing analysis.
Contrast: Gantt chart — a schedule network diagram emphasises dependency logic among activities, while a Gantt chart emphasises activities against calendar time.
Example: A hospital expansion project maps permitting, foundation work, structural steel, inspections, and interior fit-out in a network diagram to determine which path controls completion.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, schedule network diagram, page 197

Core Meaning: The improvement of a schedule to better balance timing, feasibility, resources, and project constraints.
Expansion: Schedule optimisation refers to adjusting the schedule so it better supports project objectives and constraints. It can involve resequencing work, refining logic, or applying schedule and resource optimisation techniques to improve feasibility or efficiency. PMI discusses this kind of work within schedule development and control, but does not treat Schedule optimisation as a formal standalone glossary term.
Contrast: Schedule compression — schedule optimisation is the broader effort to improve the schedule, while schedule compression specifically aims to shorten total duration.
Example: A construction team resequences interior fit-out tasks and levels specialist trades to reduce waiting time without changing the delivery date.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A measure of schedule efficiency expressed as the ratio of earned value to planned value.
Expansion: Schedule performance index, or SPI, indicates how efficiently the project is progressing against the planned schedule. A value above 1.0 suggests the project is ahead of schedule, while a value below 1.0 suggests it is behind schedule. It is commonly used in earned value analysis to support forecasting and schedule control.
Contrast: Schedule variance — SPI is a ratio of schedule efficiency; schedule variance is the numerical difference between earned value and planned value.
Example: If a project has earned 80 units of value against 100 units of planned value, the SPI is 0.8, indicating schedule inefficiency.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

Core Meaning: The activity of defining how the project schedule will be developed, managed, and controlled.
Expansion: Schedule planning establishes the approach, tools, and rules for building and maintaining the schedule before detailed sequencing and estimating begin. It covers selecting a scheduling method, defining activity and milestone conventions, and setting control thresholds for variance. In predictive work it produces a schedule management plan, while in adaptive work it favours rolling wave and lookahead techniques.
Contrast: Schedule development — schedule planning sets how scheduling will be done, while schedule development is the act of producing the actual dated schedule.
Example: Early in a construction project, the team agrees to use the critical path method with monthly rebaselining, documenting these choices as the schedule plan.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Project Schedule Management

Core Meaning: Time added within the schedule to address uncertainty and reduce the risk of delay.
Expansion: Schedule reserve is a buffer of planned time used to absorb the effect of risk or estimating uncertainty. It is commonly associated with contingency time for identified risks, though organizations may apply related reserve concepts differently in practice. In project management, schedule reserve helps improve the realism and resilience of planned completion dates.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — Schedule reserve refers specifically to time held in the schedule; contingency reserve may be expressed more broadly as time or cost set aside for identified risks.
Example: A product-launch project adds two weeks of schedule reserve before regulatory approval in case test rework is needed.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Schedule reserve

Core Meaning: A measure of schedule performance expressed as the difference between earned value and planned value.
Expansion: Schedule variance shows whether the project is ahead of or behind the approved plan at a given point in time. A positive result indicates that more value has been earned than planned, while a negative result indicates that less value has been earned than planned. It is used in earned value analysis to monitor schedule performance and support forecasting.
Contrast: Schedule performance index — schedule variance is a difference measure; schedule performance index is a ratio measure.
Example: A project planned to earn 100 units of value by month six but has earned only 85, so schedule variance shows it is behind schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / Process Groups A Practice Guide — Schedule variance, page 172

Core Meaning: The date on which an activity is planned to begin according to the approved or current project schedule.
Expansion: Scheduled start date is the planned start shown in the schedule before or aside from actual execution data. It is used to compare intent against actual start performance and forecast changes.
Contrast: Actual start date — the scheduled start date is the planned date in the schedule; the actual start date is when the work truly began.
Example: A testing activity has a scheduled start date of 15 September, but it actually starts three days later after environment setup is delayed.
Source: PMI Lexicon / Practice Standard for Scheduling — exact PMI page citation not verified from retrieved content

Core Meaning: The activity of determining when project work should occur and how tasks are arranged over time.
Expansion: Scheduling is the broader practice of placing activities, milestones, and resources into a time-based sequence. In PMI usage, it underpins schedule creation, maintenance, analysis, and control.
Contrast: Schedule development — Scheduling is the broader discipline or activity; schedule development is the formal process used to build the schedule model.
Example: A PMO scheduler updates task dates, resource calendars, and milestone deadlines each week to keep a program timeline current.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A tool that provides schedule component names, definitions, relationships, and formats to support a scheduling method.
Expansion: A scheduling tool is the software or system used to build and maintain the schedule model by applying a chosen scheduling method such as the critical path method. Combined with the scheduling method and manual inputs, it generates and updates the project schedule.
Contrast: Scheduling method - the scheduling method is the technique (e.g., critical path method), whereas the scheduling tool is the instrument that applies it.
Example: Using Microsoft Project or Primavera P6 to calculate the critical path and produce a Gantt chart.
Source: PMBOK Guide - scheduling tool

Core Meaning: The process of identifying and documenting relationships among project activities.
Expansion: Sequence Activities establishes the logical order in which activities should occur based on dependencies and constraints. It creates the basis for the project schedule network by showing predecessor and successor relationships, including leads and lags where appropriate. PMI treats it as a core scheduling process because activity lists alone do not show how work flows through time. Its outputs support schedule development, critical path analysis, and realistic planning.
Contrast: Define Activities — Define Activities identifies the work activities; Sequence Activities determines their logical order and relationships.
Example: In a facility build, the team sequences excavation before foundation work, foundation before steel erection, and steel erection before roofing to build a valid network diagram.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, Section 6.3 Sequence Activities, pp. 185-194

Core Meaning: Determining the logical order and dependencies among activities to build the schedule.
Expansion: Sequencing identifies and documents the relationships between project activities so that work is performed in a feasible order. It uses dependency types such as finish-to-start, along with leads and lags, often visualized with a precedence diagram. Correct sequencing is the foundation for calculating the critical path and overall duration.
Contrast: Sequencing arranges the order of work, whereas estimating determines how long each activity takes, and both are needed before the schedule can be modeled.
Example: Foundation work must finish before framing begins, so the planner sets a finish-to-start dependency between them.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A planning approach that breaks work into very short time horizons so teams can coordinate and adjust execution frequently.
Expansion: Short-interval planning translates higher-level plans into near-term work that can be reviewed and adjusted often. It is common in adaptive, hybrid, and execution-focused environments where conditions change quickly. It aligns closely with iterative planning and rolling wave thinking in PMI practice. The approach helps teams maintain control without overcommitting too far ahead.
Contrast: Rolling wave planning — short-interval planning focuses on the immediate execution window; rolling wave planning is the broader progressive elaboration approach.
Example: A construction supervisor plans the next two weeks of crew work each Friday based on site conditions, access constraints, and material availability.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An estimating approach that assigns one specific value as the estimate for a cost, duration, effort, or resource need.
Expansion: Single-point estimating produces one forecast number rather than a range or probability distribution. In PMI practice, it is useful when speed and simplicity are more important than expressing uncertainty explicitly. Its limitation is that it can conceal variability unless it is paired with reserves, assumptions, or risk analysis.
Contrast: Three-point estimating — single-point estimating gives one value only; three-point estimating reflects uncertainty through optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values.
Example: A project manager estimates that data-migration testing will take 10 working days and uses that single value in the draft schedule baseline.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The amount of time a schedule activity can be delayed without affecting another activity or the project finish date, depending on context.
Expansion: Slack is a common practitioner term for schedule flexibility in project work. In PMI terminology, the formal concept is usually float, but slack is often used informally with a similar meaning. It helps teams understand where delay can be absorbed and where schedule pressure is highest. Because usage varies, teams should define the term clearly when discussing schedules.
Contrast: Float — float is the PMI term; slack is the more informal practitioner term often used with the same general meaning.
Example: A testing task has two days of slack, so a short delay does not move the planned release milestone.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The amount by which a schedule, milestone, or deliverable falls behind its plan.
Expansion: Slippage quantifies how far actual progress has drifted from the baseline, acting as an early warning of schedule risk. Tracking it lets teams take corrective action before small delays compound.
Contrast: Variance — slippage specifically describes falling behind schedule; variance is any deviation (cost or schedule) from plan.
Example: A milestone planned for week 8 now forecasts week 11, a three-week slippage.
Source: Not a formal PMI glossary entry; defined for glossary completeness

Core Meaning: A scheduling relationship that reflects a preferred sequencing choice rather than a mandatory dependency.
Expansion: Soft logic describes an activity relationship based on best practice, preference, or convenience instead of a physical or contractual necessity. Project teams may change it when circumstances shift, provided the new sequence remains feasible and controlled. In PMI scheduling concepts, this is commonly contrasted with mandatory logic and is useful when optimising schedule flexibility.
Contrast: Mandatory dependency — Soft logic is discretionary and can be changed, while a mandatory dependency is inherent in the work or externally required.
Example: A construction planner initially schedules landscaping after exterior painting because it is preferred sequencing, but later moves some landscaping earlier to recover time without violating technical constraints.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — term appears as discretionary dependency; confirm exact page in Infinity

Core Meaning: Estimating the magnitude of a software effort using measures of functionality or relative complexity.
Expansion: Software sizing produces a measure of size, such as function points, lines of code, or story points, that is then converted into effort and duration estimates. Sizing supports budgeting, planning, and productivity comparison across projects. Relative sizing techniques like story points are common in agile, while function point analysis is widely used in predictive contexts.
Contrast: Differs from effort estimation, which yields person-hours; sizing first quantifies the magnitude of what is to be built.
Example: The team assigns story points to backlog items, then uses past velocity to forecast how many sprints the release will take.
Source: Software estimation practice (Function Point Analysis)

Core Meaning: A point in time associated with the start of a schedule activity.
Expansion: PMI uses start date as a schedule term for when an activity begins or is planned to begin. It can refer to several schedule states, such as actual, early, late, scheduled, or target start dates. The term is used in planning, sequencing, and schedule control.
Contrast: Project start date — start date applies to an activity; project start date applies to the project as a whole.
Example: The system-integration task has a scheduled start date of 12 August in the approved project schedule.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Start date, p. 28

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity must start before its predecessor can finish.
Expansion: Start-to-finish is the rarest of the four precedence relationships: the predecessor (from) activity cannot finish until the successor (to) activity has started. It is typically used to model handovers, such as a new shift starting before the previous one ends. Most schedules rely on finish-to-start instead.
Contrast: Finish-to-start - in finish-to-start the predecessor must finish before the successor starts, the reverse logic of start-to-finish.
Example: The new security guard's shift must begin before the outgoing guard's shift can end.
Source: PMBOK Guide - start-to-finish relationship

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has started.
Expansion: Start-to-Finish is one of PMI's precedence relationship types used in schedule network analysis. It ties the finish of the successor to the start of the predecessor rather than to the predecessor's finish. It is valid but less common than Finish-to-Start in most project schedules.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Start-to-Finish constrains when the successor can finish; Finish-to-Start constrains when the successor can start.
Example: A legacy support task cannot finish until the new operations team has started its takeover work, so the activities are linked Start-to-Finish.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition — Start-to-Finish relationship, p. 243

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until its predecessor has started.
Expansion: In a start-to-start relationship, the successor (to) activity can begin only once the predecessor (from) activity has begun, often with a lead or lag. It is used when two activities can run partly in parallel once the first is underway.
Contrast: Finish-to-start - finish-to-start ties the successor's start to the predecessor's finish, whereas start-to-start ties it to the predecessor's start.
Example: Pouring concrete can start two days after leveling the ground begins (SS with a 2-day lag).
Source: PMBOK Guide - start-to-start relationship

Core Meaning: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until a predecessor activity has started.
Expansion: A Start-to-Start relationship is a dependency type used in schedule network analysis when two activities may proceed in parallel once the first one begins. It allows overlap rather than requiring the predecessor to finish. PMI includes it as one of the standard precedence relationships used to model realistic sequencing, often with lead or lag.
Contrast: Finish-to-Start relationship — Start-to-Start allows the successor to begin after the predecessor starts; Finish-to-Start requires the predecessor to finish first.
Example: Once excavation starts on a construction project, dewatering operations can begin and continue in parallel rather than waiting for excavation to finish.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 6.3.3.1, page 157

Core Meaning: The tendency to delay starting work until just before a deadline, consuming any safety margin.
Expansion: Named in critical chain theory, student syndrome describes people putting off effort until the last possible moment, so any padding built into an estimate is wasted and a late surprise leaves no recovery time. It is one reason individual task buffers tend to disappear. Critical chain counters it by removing local safety and pooling it into shared project and feeding buffers.
Contrast: Parkinson's Law - Parkinson's law says work expands to fill available time, whereas student syndrome is specifically the delayed start that burns the buffer up front.
Example: A team member with a two-week task does nothing for the first week and a half, then scrambles and still finishes late.
Source: Critical chain / Theory of Constraints (Goldratt)

Core Meaning: A dependent activity that logically follows another activity in the project schedule.
Expansion: A successor is the activity that comes later in a dependency relationship. Its timing is linked to a predecessor through a defined logical relationship.
Contrast: Predecessor — a predecessor comes before another activity; a successor comes after it.
Example: In a construction schedule, roof installation is the successor to structural framing.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition Glossary, page 240

Core Meaning: A dependent activity that logically comes after another activity in a schedule.
Expansion: A successor activity is the later activity in a logical relationship. Its timing depends on the predecessor activity according to the defined dependency type.
Contrast: Predecessor activity — a successor activity follows the dependent relationship, while a predecessor activity comes before it.
Example: Concrete curing is the successor activity to concrete pouring in a construction schedule.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, successor activity, page 726

Core Meaning: A schedule activity that aggregates lower-level activities to present a higher-level view of work.
Expansion: A summary activity rolls up related detailed activities into one higher-level schedule line. It is used for communicating structure and status without replacing the detailed tasks underneath.
Contrast: Milestone — a summary activity represents grouped work over time, while a milestone marks a significant point or event with zero duration.
Example: A project schedule shows 'System Testing' as a summary activity that contains unit, integration, performance, and user acceptance testing activities.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, summary activity; exact page varies by edition formatting

Core Meaning: A summary schedule is a schedule presentation that shows major activities, milestones, or summary work packages at a high level rather than detailed task-level planning.
Expansion: It is used to communicate the overall timing of a project or phase to sponsors, customers, and senior stakeholders who do not need detailed activity logic. PMI treats it as a condensed view of the schedule model or schedule baseline rather than a replacement for detailed planning. It is especially useful in governance reporting, executive communication, and stage-gate reviews.
Contrast: Milestone schedule — a summary schedule can include major activities and summary elements as well as milestones, while a milestone schedule shows only key milestones.
Example: For a factory expansion, the project manager presents an executive summary schedule showing design complete, permitting, procurement, construction, commissioning, and go-live dates instead of hundreds of underlying activities.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition / prior PMBOK glossary usage for summary schedule

Core Meaning: A desired date by which a schedule activity or project is intended to finish.
Expansion: A target finish date expresses the planned or desired completion point for work. It is used as a management objective and may differ from calculated schedule dates such as early finish or late finish. Teams use it to align expectations, monitor progress, and compare actual performance against intent.
Contrast: Finish date — a target finish date is the desired completion date, while a finish date is the calculated or recorded completion point in the schedule.
Example: The team sets a target finish date of 30 September for user acceptance testing so the release can still occur in October.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — target finish date

Core Meaning: A desired date by which a schedule activity or project is intended to start.
Expansion: A target start date identifies when work is intended to begin. It is a planning objective rather than a calculated schedule result, and it helps coordinate dependencies, resources, and stakeholder expectations. Project teams use it to align readiness and timing before execution begins.
Contrast: Start date — a target start date is the desired start point, while a start date is the actual or calculated start point in the schedule.
Example: The procurement team sets a target start date of 1 March for vendor onboarding so contract approval can be completed beforehand.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — target start date

Core Meaning: A unit of work performed to complete part of an activity, deliverable, or planned effort.
Expansion: A task is a smaller piece of work that contributes to completing project work. In project practice, tasks are used to break work into manageable components for assignment, estimation, sequencing, and tracking. They help teams clarify who does what and when, especially in detailed planning and execution. The term is widely used in practice even when PMI standards more often formalise related terms such as activity or work package.
Contrast: Activity — an activity is a schedule-level element of work that may encompass multiple tasks; a task is typically a smaller practical unit used to carry out that work.
Example: For a training rollout, tasks include drafting the facilitator guide, booking the room, and sending attendee invitations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An estimating technique that uses three values to represent uncertainty in cost or duration.
Expansion: Three-point estimating uses optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic values to improve an estimate under uncertainty. It helps teams move beyond false precision from a single-number estimate. PMI places it within estimation practice for schedule and cost planning.
Contrast: Single-point estimating — three-point estimating models uncertainty with a range of values, while single-point estimating gives only one value.
Example: A project team estimates an integration task at 4 days optimistic, 7 days most likely, and 12 days pessimistic before setting the planned duration.

Source: PMBOK Guide — Three-point estimating, page 44

Core Meaning: Time in project management is the duration framework within which activities, milestones, and delivery commitments are planned, sequenced, and controlled.
Expansion: Time is a core planning and control dimension of project work because projects operate within finite durations and expected completion dates. PMI typically addresses it through schedule-related concepts such as activity duration, milestones, sequencing, and deadlines rather than as a standalone glossary term. In practice, time influences prioritisation, resource use, risk exposure, and trade-off decisions across the project lifecycle.
Contrast: Schedule — time is the underlying duration dimension; the schedule is the structured model and baseline used to manage it.
Example: In a data-center migration, the available outage window of eight hours becomes the critical time factor driving sequencing, rehearsals, and contingency planning.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A time constraint is a limiting schedule condition that restricts when work can start, finish, or be completed.
Expansion: A time constraint narrows the schedule flexibility available to the project team by imposing dates, windows, deadlines, dependencies, or regulatory timing requirements. It matters because constrained time reduces float, increases coordination pressure, and often drives trade-offs among scope, cost, quality, and risk. PMI commonly treats this through schedule constraints and schedule model logic rather than as a universally separate glossary entry.
Contrast: Deadline — a time constraint is any limiting timing condition; a deadline is one specific required completion point within that broader category.
Example: A construction project must finish noisy demolition work before a city permit window closes, making the permit date a binding time constraint.
Source: PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition, glossary term constraint; schedule-related treatment also appears in Practice Standard for Scheduling

Core Meaning: The amount of time a schedule activity can be delayed without delaying the project finish date or violating a schedule constraint.
Expansion: Total float expresses schedule flexibility for an activity within the overall network. It is derived through schedule analysis, especially critical path analysis. Activities with zero or negative total float deserve close attention because they have little or no schedule slack.
Contrast: Free float — total float is delay allowance against the overall project finish or a constraint, while free float is delay allowance without affecting a successor's early start.
Example: An activity has 3 days of total float, so it can slip by up to 3 days before the project completion date is affected.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Total float, pages 160, 161, 167, 194, 196

Core Meaning: A technique used to evaluate how different assumptions, events, or conditions could affect project outcomes.
Expansion: What-If-Scenario Analysis tests hypothetical situations to understand their effect on schedule, cost, resources, or other objectives. PMI includes it as a schedule analysis technique for exploring alternatives and possible responses before decisions are made. It is valuable when a team needs to understand the consequences of uncertainty or proposed changes.
Contrast: Sensitivity analysis — What-If-Scenario Analysis tests specific hypothetical situations, while sensitivity analysis examines which variables drive the greatest impact.
Example: A scheduler models the effect of a two-week supplier delay and compares whether resequencing work or adding resources would better protect the target finish date.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, page 222

Core Meaning: A consensus-based estimating technique that combines expert judgment, structured discussion, and iterative refinement.
Expansion: Wideband Delphi brings multiple experts together to estimate work while reducing bias and improving convergence on a shared view. PMI references it as a variation of the Delphi technique used in estimating and planning. It is especially useful when uncertainty is high and a single expert estimate would be too fragile.
Contrast: Delphi Technique — Wideband Delphi adds structured group discussion and iterative feedback to the basic Delphi approach.
Example: A software team estimates a complex integration feature by having several engineers estimate independently, discuss differences, and then re-estimate until the range narrows.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, page 416

Core Meaning: A defined time period during which a task, event, or opportunity can occur or be scheduled.
Expansion: A window is a bounded time frame in which work or access is possible. In projects it commonly appears in scheduling, deployment, testing, maintenance, and stakeholder availability constraints.
Contrast: Milestone — a window is an allowable time period, while a milestone is a specific significant point or event in time.
Example: A production release must be deployed during a Saturday night maintenance window when business systems are offline.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The days identified in a project or resource calendar as available for scheduled work.
Expansion: Working days are the days on which project work is planned to occur, excluding nonworking periods such as weekends, holidays, or calendar exceptions. They are used in schedule development, duration calculation, and resource planning because elapsed time and workable time are not always the same. The exact pattern of working days depends on the project calendar, location, contract terms, and organisational practices.
Contrast: Calendar days — working days count only scheduled work time; calendar days count every day in the elapsed period.
Example: A task estimated at five working days starts on Thursday and finishes the following Wednesday because the weekend is nonworking time in the project calendar.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A schedule characteristic indicating that an activity or event occupies no measurable time span.
Expansion: Zero duration is used in scheduling to mark a point in time rather than work that consumes time. It commonly appears in milestones and other logic points that must be shown in the network without adding elapsed duration.
Contrast: Milestone — zero duration describes the time attribute; a milestone is the event that often has that attribute.
Example: The schedule shows contract award as a zero-duration point that must occur before vendor onboarding can begin.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An activity that has no time span and is used to represent a point in time or a logical event in the schedule.
Expansion: A zero duration activity does not consume working time, but it can still be important in the schedule model. It is often used to show milestones, handoffs, or logic points that need to appear in the network. PMI distinguishes it from normal activities that have duration greater than zero.
Contrast: Milestone — a zero duration activity is a schedule element with no time span; a milestone is a significant point or event that is often represented with zero duration.
Example: A regulatory-approval point is inserted as a zero duration activity before procurement can begin.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — zero duration activity

Core Meaning: The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project finish date.
Expansion: Zero float means an activity has no scheduling flexibility. If it slips, the project finish date slips unless corrective action is taken. PMI uses float to assess schedule flexibility and identify activities that require close control.
Contrast: Total float — zero float means no delay is possible without affecting the finish date; total float is the general measure of allowable delay.
Example: A final data-migration task before cutover has zero float, so any delay pushes the planned go-live date.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — zero float

Core Meaning: A relationship timing condition in which the successor proceeds with no waiting time after the predecessor as defined by the logic link.
Expansion: Zero lag means there is no added delay between linked activities. It is the default timing in many finish-to-start relationships when no offset is needed. PMI uses lag to model waiting time, so zero lag indicates that no such delay is applied.
Contrast: Lag — zero lag has no delay between activities; lag introduces a deliberate waiting period.
Example: Once test scripts are approved, execution starts immediately with zero lag.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — zero lag
